ref: Visually-rich Document Understanding (VrDU) has attracted much research attention over the past years. Pre-trained models on a large number of document images with transformer-based backbones have led to significant performance gains in this field. The major challenge is how to fusion the different modalities (text, layout, and image) of the documents in a unified model with different pre-training tasks. This paper focuses on improving text-layout interactions and proposes a novel multi-modal pre-training model, LayoutMask. LayoutMask uses local 1D position, instead of global 1D position, as layout input and has two pre-training objectives: (1) Masked Language Modeling: predicting masked tokens with two novel masking strategies; (2) Masked Position Modeling: predicting masked 2D positions to improve layout representation learning. LayoutMask can enhance the interactions between text and layout modalities in a unified model and produce adaptive and robust multi-modal representations for downstream tasks. Experimental results show that our proposed method can achieve state-of-the-art results on a wide variety of VrDU problems, including form understanding, receipt understanding, and document image classification.

basic.text_prompt: Tu Yi from Ant Group presented a paper on the Visually-rich Document Understanding (VrDU) problem, focusing on understanding various document types like forms, receipts, and posters. The paper introduces a novel pre-trained model, LayoutMask, which uses text and layout information to enhance text-layout interactions and representations during pre-training. LayoutMask differs from previous models by using local 1D positions instead of global 1D positions, and by employing two novel masking strategies: Whole Word Masking and Layout-Aware Masking. The model also introduces a new pre-training objective, Masked Position Modeling, which helps the model learn better layout representations. Experiments show that LayoutMask using local 1D positions outperforms global 1D positions, especially in cases with misleading numbers. The paper concludes that LayoutMask can perform better due to its adaptability to such cases.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: LayoutMask: A Novel Pre-trained Model for Visually-rich Document Understanding

Abstract:
In recent years, pre-training techniques have been introduced to the Visually-rich Document Understanding (VrDU) problem, which aims to understand various types of documents such as forms, receipts, and posters. However, existing document pre-training models suffer from reading order issues. Inspired by BERT, these methods usually adopt ascending numbers (like 0, 1, 2...) to represent the global reading order of tokens in the document, which are encoded as global 1D positions. We propose a novel pre-trained model, LayoutMask, to address these issues. LayoutMask only uses text and layout information as model input, and aims to enhance the text layout interactions and layout representations learned during pre-training. It differs from previous studies in three aspects: choice of 1D position, masking strategy, and pre-training objectives. Instead of global 1D position, LayoutMask proposes to use the in-segment token orders as 1D position, which is referred to as "local 1D position". As local 1D position does not provide cross-segment orders, LayoutMask is supposed to infer global reading order by jointly using 1D position, 2D position, and semantic information, thus bringing in-depth text-layout interactions. To further promote such interactions, we equip the commonly used pre-training objective, Masked Language Modeling, with two novel masking strategies: Whole Word Masking and Layout-Aware Masking. The Masked Language Modeling task is the most essential and commonly used pre-training task in multi-modal pre-training. Following the Whole Word Masking strategy, we set masks at word-level instead of token-level, which is much more challenging. When using Whole Word Masking, the semantic relations between masked and unmasked tokens of the same words are eliminated, so the model has to find more context to predict masked words, which can promote text-layout interactions. In Layout-Aware Masking strategy, the first and last words of each segment have a higher probability to be masked, so the model has to pay more attention to finding their contexts in the preceding or succeeding segment, thus promoting learning cross-segment orders. We also designed a new pre-training objective, Masked Position Modeling. It has a symmetric pre-training objective: recovering randomly masked 2D positions during pre-training. The MPM task is very similar to the cloze test, where a group of randomly selected words is supposed to be refilled at the right positions in the original documents.

It differs from previous studies in three aspects: choice of 1D position, masking strategy, and pre-training objectives. The model uses local 1D position instead of global 1D position, which is referred to as "local 1D position". The model also introduces two novel masking strategies: Whole Word Masking and Layout-Aware Masking. The Masked Language Modeling task is the most essential and commonly used pre-training task in multi-modal pre-training. The model also introduces a new pre-training objective, Masked Position Modeling. The performance of LayoutMask using different layout information is compared in the experiments. The model performs better with local 1D position on both FUNSD and SROIE, and falls a little behind on CORD. The performance gap between local and global mainly comes from the entity "Total". The model is more adaptive to such cases and can perform better.

detailed.text_prompt:
Tu Yi from Ant Group presented a paper on the Visually-rich Document Understanding (VrDU) problem, focusing on understanding various types of documents like forms, receipts, and posters. The paper introduces a novel pre-trained model, LayoutMask, which aims to enhance text layout interactions and representations during pre-training. The model uses text and layout information as input and differs from previous studies in three aspects: choice of 1D position, masking strategy, and pre-training objectives.

Instead of using global 1D position, LayoutMask uses local 1D position, which represents the in-segment token orders. This local 1D position does not provide cross-segment orders, so the model infers global reading order by jointly using 1D position, 2D position, and semantic information. The model also introduces two novel masking strategies: Whole Word Masking and Layout-Aware Masking. Whole Word Masking sets masks at word-level instead of token-level, eliminating semantic relations between masked and unmasked tokens of the same words. Layout-Aware Masking masks the first and last words of each segment, promoting learning cross-segment orders.

The paper also introduces a new pre-training objective, Masked Position Modeling, which involves recovering randomly masked 2D positions during pre-training. The model has to find the context for each word based on semantic relations and infer with 2D position clues from a spatial perspective. The joint learning process with both semantic and spatial inference can promote text-layout interactions and help the model learn better layout representations.

In experiments, LayoutMask using local 1D position outperformed global 1D position on both FUNSD and SROIE datasets, and fell slightly behind on CORD. The performance gap between local and global mainly comes from the entity "Total", while other entities have similar F1 scores. The paper concludes that using local 1D can perform better since it is more adaptive to such cases. For more details, the paper and posters are available for reference.

Title: LayoutMask: A Novel Pre-Trained Model for Visually-Rich Document Understanding

Abstract: This paper presents LayoutMask, a novel pre-trained model designed to address the challenges of reading order issues in document understanding tasks. The model utilizes text and layout information as inputs, aiming to enhance text-layout interactions and improve layout representations during pre-training. LayoutMask differs from previous studies in three aspects: choice of 1D position, masking strategy, and pre-training objectives. The proposed model employs local 1D position, whole word masking, layout-aware masking, and masked position modeling as its pre-training objectives. The experiments demonstrate that LayoutMask outperforms existing models using different layout information, particularly in the FUNSD and SROIE datasets. The paper concludes that LayoutMask is more adaptive to cases with misleading numbers and can perform better in document understanding tasks.

ref: To adapt text summarization to the multilingual world, previous work proposes multi-lingual summarization (MLS) and cross-lingual summarization (CLS). However, these two tasks have been studied separately due to the different definitions, which limits the compatible and systematic research on both of them. In this paper, we aim to unify MLS and CLS into a more general setting, i.e., many-to-many summarization (M2MS), where a single model could process documents in any language and generate their summaries also in any language. As the first step towards M2MS, we conduct preliminary studies to show that M2MS can better transfer task knowledge across different languages than MLS and CLS. Furthermore, we propose Pisces, a pre-trained M2MS model that learns language modeling, cross-lingual ability and summarization ability via three-stage pre-training. Experimental results indicate that our Pisces significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines, especially in the zero-shot directions, where there is no training data from the source-language documents to the target-language summaries.

basic.text_prompt: Jiaan presents their work, "Towards Unifying Multi-Lingual and Cross-Lingual Summarization," which aims to create a single summarization model capable of processing documents in any source language and generating summaries in any target language, termed many-to-many summarization. They conducted preliminary studies comparing this approach with previous multilingual and cross-lingual summarization methods, finding that many-to-many summarization better transfers task knowledge across languages. They also introduced PISCES, a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model, which outperforms existing models in experiments and human studies.

The work includes preliminary studies and proposes PISCES, a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model, which outperforms existing models like mBART-50 and mT5.

detailed.f_audio_prompt: Hello everyone, I'm Jiaan. I'm so excited to present our work, "Towards Unifying Multi-Lingual and Cross-Lingual Summarization". This is a joint work with Fandong, Duo, Yunlong, Zhixu, Jianfeng, and Jie. First, let me summarize our contributions in this work. We unify previous multilingual summarization and cross-lingual summarization into a more general setting named many-to-many summarization. Many-to-many summarization aims to build one single summarization model to process a document in any source language and generate its summary in any target language. We also conduct preliminary studies to provide deeper analyses among multilingual summarization, cross-lingual summarization, and our many-to-many summarization. And we find that many-to-many summarization could help the summarization model better transfer task knowledge across different languages than previous multilingual summarization and cross-lingual summarization. Moreover, we proposed PISCES, a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model that learns language modeling, cross-lingual ability, and summarization ability through a carefully designed three-stage pre-training. Next, I would like to show you the difference between previous multilingual summarization, cross-lingual summarization, and our many-to-many summarization. Look at this figure. Given a document in your source language, the multilingual summarization model aims to generate its summary also in the source language, and the cross-lingual summarization model aims to generate its summary in a different target language. In other words, the input language and output language are the same on the settings of multi-Lingual summarization, while they are different on the settings of cross-lingual summarization. And our many-to-many summarization combines two previous tasks into a more general setting that the model can summarize the document in any language into the corresponding summary, also in any language. To compare cross-lingual summarization, multilingual summarization, and our many-to-many summarization, we conduct a preliminary experiment on the widely-used WikiLingua dataset. The samples used in the experiments includes English, French, Hindi, Chinese, Thai, and Turkish. We trained the following four models with the same backbone, that is mBART-50. First, mBART ONE: we separately train several mBART-50 models, each of which is built and evaluated in one single direction. Second, mBART U-CLS: We train a unified model with all cross-lingual samples, and test the model in all directions. Third, mBART MLS: We train one unified model with monolingual samples in all directions, and test the model also in all directions. Lastly, we train mBART Many-to-Many Summarization with the new settings introduced by our work, where the model is both trained and evaluated in all directions. This table shows the result of our preliminary experiments. We can see that the multilingual model trained in the many-to-many summarization setting can better transfer task knowledge across different languages than the settings of multilingual summarization, cross-lingual summarization, and unified cross-lingual summarization. Moreover, since we are the first to use many-to-many summarization, we also propose a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model named PISCES. Our PISCES is trained through a carefully designed three-stage pre-training. Specifically, meta pre-training requires a model to generate original sentences based on the noisy counterparts, and cross-lingual pre-training generates the sentences in the target language based on the noisy parallel sentences in the different source language. And task-specific pre-training utilizes pseudo many-to-many summarization samples to train the model. For more details, please see our paper. The experimental results show that our PISCES outperforms various baselines, including mBART-50 and mT5. We also conduct ablation studies to verify the effectiveness of each training stage and conduct human studies to show the superiority of our PISCES. Thus, please don't forget to check out our paper. Thanks for listening.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Introduction:
Jiaan, along with his colleagues Fandong, Duo, Yunlong, Zhixu, Jianfeng, and Jie, presents their work titled "Towards Unifying Multi-Lingual and Cross-Lingual Summarization". The main goal of this research is to develop a single summarization model that can process a document in any source language and generate its summary in any target language, which is referred to as many-to-many summarization. The team aims to unify previous multilingual summarization and cross-lingual summarization into this more general setting.

Contributions:
The researchers have made several contributions in this work, including:
1. Unifying multilingual summarization and cross-lingual summarization into a more general setting called many-to-many summarization.
2. Conducting preliminary studies to analyze the differences between multilingual summarization, cross-lingual summarization, and many-to-many summarization.
3. Proposing PISCES, a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model that learns language modeling, cross-lingual ability, and summarization ability through a carefully designed three-stage pre-training.

Preliminary Studies:
The team conducted preliminary studies to provide deeper analyses among multilingual summarization, cross-lingual summarization, and many-to-many summarization. They found that many-to-many summarization could help the summarization model better transfer task knowledge across different languages than previous multilingual summarization and cross-lingual summarization.

Many-to-Many Summarization:
Many-to-many summarization aims to build a single summarization model that can process a document in any source language and generate its summary in any target language. The team conducted a preliminary experiment on the WikiLingua dataset, which includes samples in English, French, Hindi, Chinese, Thai, and Turkish. They trained four models with the same backbone, mBART-50, and compared the results. The results showed that the many-to-many summarization model outperformed the other settings.

PISCES:
The team proposed PISCES, a pre-trained many-to-many summarization model that learns language modeling, cross-lingual ability, and summarization ability through a carefully designed three-stage pre-training. The model was trained and evaluated in all directions, and the experimental results showed that PISCES outperformed various baselines, including mBART-50 and mT5. The team also conducted ablation studies and human studies to verify the effectiveness of each training stage and show the superiority of PISCES.

Conclusion:
In conclusion, Jiaan and his colleagues have made significant contributions to the field of summarization by developing a many-to-many summarization model that can process a document in any source language and generate its summary in any target language. Their work, PISCES, outperforms various baselines and demonstrates the effectiveness of their approach. The team encourages readers to check out their paper for more details.

ref: Large neural networks can now generate jokes, but do they really “understand” humor? We challenge AI models with three tasks derived from the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest: matching a joke to a cartoon, identifying a winning caption, and explaining why a winning caption is funny. These tasks encapsulate progressively more sophisticated aspects of “understanding” a cartoon; key elements are the complex, often surprising relationships between images and captions and the frequent inclusion of indirect and playful allusions to human experience and culture. We investigate both multimodal and language-only models: the former are challenged with the cartoon images directly, while the latter are given multifaceted descriptions of the visual scene to simulate human-level visual understanding. We find that both types of models struggle at all three tasks. For example, our best multimodal models fall 30 accuracy points behind human performance on the matching task, and, even when provided ground-truth visual scene descriptors, human-authored explanations are preferred head-to-head over the best machine-authored ones (few-shot GPT-4) in more than 2/3 of cases. We release models, code, leaderboard, and corpus, which includes newly-gathered annotations describing the image’s locations/entities, what’s unusual in the scene, and an explanation of the joke.

ref: Adaptive inference is a simple method for reducing inference costs. The method works by maintaining multiple classifiers of different capacities, and allocating resources to each test instance according to its difficulty. In this work, we compare the two main approaches for adaptive inference, Early-Exit and Multi-Model, when training data is limited. First, we observe that for models with the same architecture and size, individual Multi-Model classifiers outperform their Early-Exit counterparts by an average of 2.3%. We show that this gap is caused by Early-Exit classifiers sharing model parameters during training, resulting in conflicting gradient updates of model weights. We find that despite this gap, Early-Exit still provides a better speed-accuracy trade-off due to the overhead of the Multi-Model approach. To address these issues, we propose SWEET (Separating Weights for Early-Exit Transformers) an Early-Exit fine-tuning method that assigns each classifier its own set of unique model weights, not updated by other classifiers. We compare SWEET’s speed-accuracy curve to standard Early-Exit and Multi-Model baselines and find that it outperforms both methods at fast speeds while maintaining comparable scores to Early- Exit at slow speeds. Moreover, SWEET individual classifiers outperform Early-Exit ones by 1.1% on average. SWEET enjoys the benefits of both methods, paving the way for further reduction of inference costs in NLP.

basic.text_prompt: Daniel Rotem presented his work on improving adaptive inference in low-resource settings, focusing on the SWEET (Separating Weights in Early Exit Transformers) method. Adaptive inference methods, such as Multi Model and Early Exit, aim to reduce inference time of large language models by using simpler models for easier samples. Multi Model involves running multiple models sequentially, while Early Exit uses classifiers at intermediate layers to halt computation early. Rotem's research found that conflicting gradients in Early Exit models can degrade performance, and he introduced SWEET, a method that trains each layer with updates only from the following classifier, avoiding this issue. SWEET showed promising results, outperforming both Multi Model and Early Exit in some cases, and motivated further research in fine-tuning algorithms for Early Exit architectures.

The results show that SWEET outperforms both methods in terms of speed and accuracy, motivating future research and fine-tuning algorithms for Early Exit architectures.

formal.text_prompt: Daniel Rotem presented his work, "Finding the SWEET Spot: Analysis and Improvement of Adaptive Inference in Low Resource Settings," in Professor Roy Schwartz's lab at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. The work focuses on adaptive inference, a method to reduce the inference time of large language models by using low-capacity models for easy samples. The two most common adaptive inference methods are Multi Model and Early Exit. Multi Model is more versatile but expensive to store and suffers from overhead. Early Exit is faster and memory efficient but can lead to lower performance due to conflicting gradients. Rotem's research found that Multi Model classifiers outperformed Early Exit classifiers by an average of 2.3%. To address this, Rotem introduced the SWEET method, which trains an Early Exit architecture where each layer receives updates only from the following classifier, avoiding the conflicting gradient problem. The SWEET method closed most of the gap between Early Exit and Multi Model and outperformed both methods in fast speeds. The work shows the existence of conflicting gradients in Early Exit training and introduces the SWEET method, motivating future research and fine-tuning algorithms tailored to the Early Exit architecture.

detailed.text_prompt:
Daniel Rotem presented his work, "Finding the SWEET Spot: Analysis and Improvement of Adaptive Inference in Low Resource Settings," in Professor Roy Schwartz's lab at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. The work focuses on adaptive inference, a method that reduces the inference time of large language models by using low-capacity models for easy samples. The two most common adaptive inference methods are Multi Model and Early Exit. Multi Model stores multiple models, each with a classifier at the end, and runs them sequentially until a classifier decides to halt the computation. Early Exit, on the other hand, uses multiple classifiers at intermediate transformer layers, and halts the computation when a classifier decides to do so. 

The presentation highlighted the pros and cons of each method. Multi Model is more versatile and easily extended, but it is expensive to store and suffers from overhead. Early Exit is faster, memory efficient, but model parameters are shared amongst all classifiers, which can lead to lower performance due to conflicting gradients. 

Rotem's team compared individual Early Exit models' classifiers with separate Multi Model classifiers, which are truncated versions of the BERT pre-trained language model. They found that Multi Model classifiers outperformed Early Exit by an average of 2.3%, both for BERT-base and for BERT-large. 

To address the conflicting gradient problem, Rotem's team introduced the SWEET (Separating Weights in Early Exit Transformers) method, which trains an Early Exit architecture where each layer receives updates only from the following classifier. This method avoids the conflicting gradient problem and closes most of the gap between Early Exit and Multi Model. 

In conclusion, Rotem's work shows the existence of conflicting gradients in Early Exit training process, conducts the first fair comparison of Early Exit and Multi Model adaptive inference methods, and introduces the SWEET method, which motivates future research and fine-tuning algorithms tailored to the Early Exit architecture.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
Daniel Rotem presented his work on "Finding the SWEET Spot: Analysis and Improvement of Adaptive Inference in Low Resource Settings" in Professor Roy Schwartz's lab at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. The main focus of the presentation was on adaptive inference, a method to reduce the inference time of large language models by using low-capacity models for easy samples. The two most common adaptive inference methods discussed were Multi Model and Early Exit.

Multi Model involves storing multiple models together, each with a classifier at the end. These models are trained separately on the entire training set and run sequentially during inference until a classifier decides to halt the computation. The pros of Multi Model include versatility and ease of extension, while the cons are the high storage cost and overhead due to running samples through all previous models without using their output.

Early Exit, on the other hand, involves fitting multiple classifiers to the model following intermediate transformer layers. These classifiers are trained together, and for inference, a sample is run through the model until a classifier decides to halt, saving computation. The pros of Early Exit are faster inference and memory efficiency, while the cons include shared model parameters among classifiers, which can lead to lower performance due to conflicting gradients.

The presentation highlighted the existence of conflicting gradients in Early Exit training and introduced the SWEET (Separating Weights in Early Exit Transformers) method. SWEET is a fine-tuning method for Early Exit architectures that trains each layer to receive updates only from the following classifier, thus avoiding the conflicting gradient problem. The results showed that SWEET closes most of the gap between Early Exit and Multi Model, and in some cases, it even outperforms both methods.

In conclusion, the presentation provided a fair comparison of Early Exit and Multi Model adaptive inference methods, introduced the SWEET method, and motivated future research and fine-tuning algorithms tailored to the Early Exit architecture.

Multi Model is more versatile but has overhead and storage costs, while Early Exit is faster and memory-efficient but can suffer from conflicting gradients due to shared model parameters. The SWEET method addresses this issue by training each layer to receive updates only from the following classifier, thus avoiding conflicting gradients. The results show that SWEET outperforms both Multi Model and Early Exit in terms of speed and accuracy, especially for fast inference speeds. The work highlights the existence of conflicting gradients in Early Exit training and provides a fair comparison between Multi Model and Early Exit methods.

ref: Existing research on multimodal relation extraction (MRE) faces two co-existing challenges, internal-information over-utilization and external-information under-exploitation. To combat that, we propose a novel framework that simultaneously implements the idea of internal-information screening and external-information exploiting. First, we represent the fine-grained semantic structures of the input image and text with the visual and textual scene graphs, which are further fused into a unified cross-modal graph (CMG). Based on CMG, we perform structure refinement with the guidance of the graph information bottleneck principle, actively denoising the less-informative features. Next, we perform topic modeling over the input image and text, incorporating latent multimodal topic features to enrich the contexts. On the benchmark MRE dataset, our system outperforms the current best model significantly. With further in-depth analyses, we reveal the great potential of our method for the MRE task.

The research addresses two main issues: over-utilization of internal information and under-exploitation of external information. To solve these, Wu's team proposes a Graph Information Bottleneck principle-guided feature refinement and enriches the model with multimodal topic information. Their method, which includes a unified cross-modal graph (CMG) and an attention operation for topic integration, shows improved performance over existing models. The research also includes an ablation study and experiments that demonstrate the importance of both internal and external information in relation extraction. The team concludes that their approach of simultaneously subtracting and adding information is effective for multimodal relation extraction.

The proposed method addresses two main issues: internal-information over-utilization and external-information under-exploitation. To solve these problems, Wu's team proposes a Graph Information Bottleneck principle-guided feature refinement and considers multimodal topic information as additional semantic supplementary. The method consists of five parts, including representing text and image with corresponding visual scene graph and textual scene graph, merging the visual scene graph and textual scene graph into one unified backbone cross-modal graph (CMG), screening the initial CMG structures by fine-grainedly filtering nodes and adjusting edges in CMG, and enriching the compressed CMG features with multimodal topic features. The proposed method achieves significant improvements over existing best models on the benchmarks.

detailed.f_audio_prompt: Hello everyone, my name is Shengqiong Wu, a PhD student at NUS. I'm glad to introduce our work about multimodal relation extraction. Relation extraction is a widely explored task. It aims to determine the semantic relation between entities in a given text. However, in some realistic scenarios, such as in social media, the data is often in various forms and modalities rather than just pure text. So if you just look at the text, you may find that it lacks sufficient context to understand some ambiguous or multi-context word. So multimodal relation extraction has been introduced recently. Additional visual sources are added to the textual RE. For example, with the support of visual evidence such as "Bachelor", "Gown", "Cap", we can easily infer the relationship between JFK and Harvard is "graduated at". However, there are still some problems. One problem is internal-information over-utilization. This is because when inferring the relationship between two entities, only parts of the text are useful. Moreover, not all and always the visual sources play positive roles for the target task. Therefore, we argue that a fine-grained information pruning over two modalities is needed. The other problem is external-information under-exploitation. This is because, although compensating the text inputs with visual sources, there can still be an information deficiency, especially when the visual features serve less or even negative utility. In this way, we think more external information should be considered, such as topic information. To address these two issues, we firstly propose a Graph Information Bottleneck principle-guided feature refinement. And then we consider taking multimodal topic information as additional semantic supplementary to enrich the overall context. This is the whole framework of our proposed method. It consists of five parts. Firstly, we represent the text and image with the corresponding visual scene graph and the textual scene graph. And then we consider merging the visual scene graph and the textual scene graph into one unified backbone cross-modal graph, named CMG. Next, we screen the initial CMG structures by fine-grainedly filtering nodes and adjusting edges in CMG. To ensure the above adjustments of CMG is correct, our graph information bottleneck is leveraged to guide optimization. Then we enrich the compressed CMG features with the multimodal topic features. We retrieve the associated top-L textual and visual topic keywords, and then an attention operation is used to integrate the multimodal topic words to enrich the overall context.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Introduction:
Shengqiong Wu, a PhD student at NUS, introduces their work on multimodal relation extraction. The task aims to determine the semantic relation between entities in a given text, but in realistic scenarios, data is often in various forms and modalities. Multimodal relation extraction has been introduced recently to address this issue.

Problem 1: Internal-information over-utilization
The problem arises when inferring the relationship between two entities, only parts of the text are useful, and not all visual sources play a positive role for the target task. This leads to an over-utilization of internal information.

Problem 2: External-information under-exploitation
Although compensating the text inputs with visual sources, there can still be an information deficiency, especially when the visual features serve less or even negative utility. This leads to under-utilization of external information.

Proposed Solution:
To address these issues, Wu proposes a Graph Information Bottleneck principle-guided feature refinement and considers taking multimodal topic information as additional semantic supplementary to enrich the overall context.

Framework:
The proposed method consists of five parts:
1. Representing the text and image with corresponding visual scene graph and textual scene graph.
2. Merging the visual scene graph and textual scene graph into one unified backbone cross-modal graph (CMG).
3. Screening the initial CMG structures by fine-grainedly filtering nodes and adjusting edges in CMG.
4. Enriching the compressed CMG features with multimodal topic features.
5. Integrating multimodal topic words to enrich the overall context.

Evaluation:
The proposed method is evaluated on a widely used MRE dataset. The results show that leveraging visual features can obtain higher performances compared to the text-based method. All proposed methods outperform multimodal baselines.

Ablation Study:
The ablation study reveals that information screening and compensating both contribute to task performances. The scene graph is beneficial for structural modeling of multimodal inputs. Removing the scene graph decreases performances.

Conclusion:
Wu introduces a novel idea of simultaneous information subtraction and addition for multimodal relation extraction. The proposed system achieves significant improvements over existing best models on the benchmarks.

The research demonstrates that this approach significantly improves MRE performance over existing models, highlighting the importance of both internal and external information in extracting relations from multimodal data.

ref: Open domain question answering (ODQA) is a longstanding task aimed at answering factual questions from a large knowledge corpus without any explicit evidence in natural language processing (NLP). Recent works have predominantly focused on improving the answering accuracy and have achieved promising progress. However, higher accuracy often requires more memory consumption and inference latency, which might not necessarily be efficient enough for direct deployment in the real world. Thus, a trade-off between accuracy, memory consumption and processing speed is pursued. In this paper, we will survey recent advancements in the efficiency of ODQA models and conclude core techniques for achieving efficiency. Additionally, we will provide a quantitative analysis of memory cost, query speed, accuracy, and overall performance comparison. Our goal is to keep scholars informed of the latest advancements and open challenges in ODQA efficiency research and contribute to the further development of ODQA efficiency.

basic.text_prompt: Zhang Qin, a master's student from Shenzhen University, presented their work, "A Survey for Efficient Open Domain Question Answering," at ACL 2023. The work focuses on improving open-domain question answering systems, which are challenged by large Wikipedia corpora, slow inference speeds due to index searching, and the use of large language models. The team aims to create systems with smaller memory costs, faster inference, and comparable performance. They discuss efficient tactics such as approximate nearest neighbor search, skip reading, and reducing index size. They also compare retrieval-only and generator-only systems, concluding that retrieval-only systems are suitable for real-time feedback, while retrieval and reader systems are better for trade-offs. Future work includes deploying these systems on low-power devices and considering new evaluation metrics.

The work focuses on the challenges and potential solutions for efficient open-domain question answering systems. The two-stage model, proposed by Danqi Chen in 2017, is the mainstream framework, which involves a retrieval stage and a reader stage. The retrieval stage uses a question encoder and a document encoder to retrieve evidence contexts from a Wikipedia corpus. The reader stage then uses these contexts to reason out the answer. The challenges of this approach include the large size of the Wikipedia corpus, the size of the index file, and the large number of parameters in the language models. To address these challenges, Zhang Qin and their team propose several solutions, including approximate nearest neighbor search, skip reading, index size reduction, and model size reduction. Future work includes deploying these systems in low-power devices and considering new evaluation metrics.

ref: Current image generation models struggle to reliably produce well-formed visual text. In this paper, we investigate a key contributing factor: popular text-to-image models lack character-level input features, making it much harder to predict a word’s visual makeup as a series of glyphs. To quantify this effect, we conduct a series of experiments comparing character-aware vs. character-blind text encoders. In the text-only domain, we find that character-aware models provide large gains on a novel spelling task (WikiSpell). Applying our learnings to the visual domain, we train a suite of image generation models, and show that character-aware variants outperform their character-blind counterparts across a range of novel text rendering tasks (our DrawText benchmark). Our models set a much higher state-of-the-art on visual spelling, with 30+ point accuracy gains over competitors on rare words, despite training on far fewer examples.

basic.text_prompt:
Dan Garrette discusses the challenges faced by text-image models, particularly in rendering text accurately. He focuses on the Imagen model, which uses a T5-XXL encoder and a diffusion model to generate images from text. Garrette highlights that while these models can produce high-quality images, they often struggle with spelling, especially for complex inputs. He explains that T5's SentencePiece tokenization breaks down text into subword IDs, which can hinder accurate spelling. ByT5, which receives individual bytes, performs better at spelling due to its access to character-level information.

Garrette's experiments reveal that T5's spelling accuracy is low, with even the largest model achieving under 70% accuracy. In contrast, ByT5 excels at spelling across all scales. To improve text rendering, Garrette augments the Imagen model with ByT5-small, which only slightly increases the parameter count but significantly enhances the model's ability to spell correctly. This approach, while not perfect, shows promise in improving text rendering in image generation models.

They compare T5 with PaLM models, which perform better but are impractical due to their size and training data. ByT5, which uses byte-level input, excels in spelling. The team proposes enhancing Imagen by incorporating ByT5's character-level representation, improving text rendering without significantly increasing model size.

ref: We present LLM-Blender, an ensembling framework designed to attain consistently superior performance by leveraging the diverse strengths of multiple open-source large language models (LLMs). Our framework consists of two modules: PairRanker and GenFuser, addressing the observation that optimal LLMs for different examples can significantly vary. PairRanker employs a specialized pairwise comparison method to distinguish subtle differences between candidate outputs. It jointly encodes the input text and a pair of candidates, using cross-attention encoders to determine the superior one. Our results demonstrate that PairRanker exhibits the highest correlation with ChatGPT-based ranking. Then, GenFuser aims to merge the top-ranked candidates, generating an improved output by capitalizing on their strengths and mitigating their weaknesses. To facilitate large-scale evaluation, we introduce a benchmark dataset, MixInstruct, which is a mixture of multiple instruction datasets featuring oracle pairwise comparisons. Our LLM-Blender significantly outperform individual LLMs and baseline methods across various metrics, establishing a substantial performance gap.

basic.text_prompt: The paper "LLM-Blender" introduces a novel ensemble learning framework designed to enhance the performance of large language models (LLMs) by leveraging pairwise ranking and generative fusion. Developed by a team from AI2 and USC, the framework addresses the limitation of relying on a single top-performing model for all input examples, as it often fails to capture the nuances of specific inputs. LLM-Blender operates in two stages: first, it uses a pairwise ranking module, PairRanker, to compare the outputs of multiple LLMs for a given input, and second, it selects the top K models to generate a final output through a sequence-to-sequence model. The framework was evaluated using a new dataset, MixInstruct, and demonstrated superior performance over existing methods, particularly in 68% and 76% of examples for two top models, Open Assistant and Vicuna. The authors released a unified codebase for further research and evaluation.

formal.text_prompt:
The paper "LLM-Blender" introduces a novel ensemble learning framework designed to enhance the performance of large language models (LLMs) by leveraging pairwise ranking and generative fusion. Developed by a team from AI2 and USC, the framework addresses the limitations of relying on a single top-performing model for all input examples, as it often fails to capture the nuanced differences between various models' outputs. The LLM-Blender framework consists of two main components: PairRanker and GenFuser.

PairRanker is a pairwise comparison module that evaluates and ranks multiple LLMs based on their performance for a given input. It encodes pairs of candidate outputs alongside the input, allowing for a more detailed analysis of the candidates' relative quality. The PairRanker uses a cross-attention mechanism, such as RoBERTa, to distinguish which candidate is better for the input. The results are aggregated using a matrix of comparison logits, with the max logits method being the most effective.

GenFuser then selects the top K candidates (e.g., top three) and uses them as input to a sequence-to-sequence model, which generates the final output by fusing the top-ranked candidates. This two-stage process significantly improves the overall performance of the ensemble.

The paper also introduces MixInstruct, a new dataset comprising instruction datasets and candidates from 11 open-source LLMs, to evaluate the effectiveness of the LLM-Blender framework. The results demonstrate that LLM-Blender outperforms the top two models, Open Assistant and Vicuna, on various metrics, including BERTScore, BLUERT, BARTScore, and human judgment from ChatGPT. The framework's performance is shown to be superior in 68% and 76% of examples for Open Assistant and Vicuna, respectively.

In conclusion, the LLM-Blender framework offers a simple yet effective solution for ensemble learning, significantly enhancing the performance of LLMs by considering multiple models' outputs and generating superior results. The paper also provides a unified codebase for future research and evaluation.

detailed.text_prompt:
The paper "LLM-Blender" introduces a novel ensemble learning framework designed to enhance the performance of large language models (LLMs) by leveraging pairwise ranking and generative fusion. The authors, Yuchen Lin and their team from AI2 and USC, argue that relying on a single top-performing LLM for all input examples is suboptimal, as the optimal model can vary significantly across different inputs. To address this, they propose the LLM-Blender framework, which consists of two main components: PairRanker and GenFuser.

PairRanker is a pairwise comparison module that evaluates the relative quality of different LLM outputs for a given input. It does this by encoding pairs of candidate outputs alongside the input and using a cross-attention mechanism, such as RoBERTa, to determine which candidate is better. The results are aggregated into a comparison matrix, and the top K candidates are selected for the next stage.

GenFuser is the second component, which takes the top K candidates and uses a sequence-to-sequence model to generate a final output by fusing the information from these candidates. This fusion process aims to produce a more accurate and contextually appropriate response than any single model could achieve on its own.

The authors also introduce MixInstruct, a new dataset that includes candidates from 11 open-source LLMs, to evaluate the effectiveness of their framework. They demonstrate that LLM-Blender significantly outperforms the top two models, Open Assistant and Vicuna, on various metrics, including BERTScore, BLUERT, BARTScore, and human judgment from ChatGPT. The results show that Blender's performance is superior in 68% and 76% of examples for Open Assistant and Vicuna, respectively.

In conclusion, the LLM-Blender framework is presented as a simple yet effective method for improving the performance of LLMs through ensemble learning. The authors highlight the potential of their approach to consistently outperform individual models and encourage further research and evaluation using the provided MixInstruct dataset and unified codebase.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
The LLM-Blender framework, developed by a team from AI2 and USC, introduces an innovative approach to ensemble learning for large language models. The key idea behind this framework is the use of pairwise ranking and generative fusion, which allows for the selection of the optimal model for a given input example. The framework consists of two sub-modules: PairRanker and GenFuser.

PairRanker is a pairwise comparison module that compares all candidates (large language models) for a given input example. It uses a cross-attention module, such as RoBERTa, to learn the subtle differences between each pair of candidates. The module generates a comparison matrix, where each element represents the comparison logits for candidate I being better than candidate J. The PairRanker then aggregates the results using the max logits method, which is the best solution for efficiency. However, if efficiency is a concern, the bubble sort algorithm can also be used.

GenFuser is the second sub-module that takes the top three candidates ranked by PairRanker and uses them as input to a sequence-to-sequence model for learning and inference. This fusion model outputs the final result for the input example by fusing the top three candidates. The GenFuser module allows for the generation of better outputs than using any single model for all inputs.

The LLM-Blender framework was evaluated using a new dataset called MixInstruct, which consists of existing instruction datasets and candidates from 11 open-source large language models. The framework was found to be more effective than other methods, such as Open Assistant and Vicuna, in various metrics. The results showed that Blender's performance can beat the top two models in 68% and 76% of examples, respectively.

In conclusion, the LLM-Blender framework is a simple and effective ensemble learning framework for large language models. It consists of two sub-modules: PairRanker and GenFuser, which work together to select the optimal model for a given input example and generate better outputs. The framework has been shown to improve performance in various metrics and is a promising approach for ensemble learning.

ref: Selective rationales and counterfactual examples have emerged as two effective, complementary classes of interpretability methods for analyzing and training NLP models. However, prior work has not explored how these methods can be integrated to combine their complementary advantages. We overcome this limitation by introducing CREST (ContRastive Edits with Sparse raTionalization), a joint framework for selective rationalization and counterfactual text generation, and show that this framework leads to improvements in counterfactual quality, model robustness, and interpretability. First, CREST generates valid counterfactuals that are more natural than those produced by previous methods, and subsequently can be used for data augmentation at scale, reducing the need for human-generated examples. Second, we introduce a new loss function that leverages CREST counterfactuals to regularize selective rationales and show that this regularization improves both model robustness and rationale quality, compared to methods that do not leverage CREST counterfactuals. Our results demonstrate that CREST successfully bridges the gap between selective rationales and counterfactual examples, addressing the limitations of existing methods and providing a more comprehensive view of a model’s predictions.

basic.text_prompt: Marquin Treviso presented a work called "CREST: A Joint Framework for Rationalization and Counterfactual Text Generation", in collaboration with Alexis Ross, Nuno Guerreiro, and André Martins. The work combines selective rationalization and counterfactual generation to produce valid, fluent, and diverse counterfactuals. The framework, CREST, generates counterfactuals by masking the original input and using a masked language model to fill in the masked response with new tokens. The quality of the counterfactuals was evaluated using human evaluation, and the results showed that CREST counterfactuals were more valid and natural than those produced by other methods. The framework also showed that it could improve downstream models and produce more plausible rationales. The work was tested on IMDB, SNLI, and other datasets, and the results were promising. The paper and code for the work are available for further information.

It outperforms other methods in various datasets, producing plausible and diverse counterfactuals.

formal.text_prompt: Marquin Treviso presented a work titled "CREST: A Joint Framework for Rationalization and Counterfactual Text Generation", in collaboration with Alexis Ross, Nuno Guerreiro, and André Martins. The work combines selective rationalization and counterfactual generation to produce valid, fluent, and diverse counterfactuals. The framework, CREST, consists of two components: a rationalizer model that generates counterfactuals and a predictor that receives the rationale as input for a decision. The counterfactuals are evaluated by humans and compared to manual counterfactuals and those generated by MiCE. The results show that CREST counterfactuals are more valid and natural than those produced by MiCE. The framework also includes a new regularization term that encourages the new rationales to be similar to those originally generated by CREST-Generation. The results show that CREST-Rationalization achieves the top result on IMDB itself, and outperforms other methods on out-of-domain datasets. The rationales generated by CREST are also found to be more plausible and have a higher counterfactual simulability than those produced by other methods. The work suggests that using counterfactuals during training can lead to plausible explanations that focus on the contrasting parts of the input.

ref: Multilingual Machine Translation promises to improve translation quality between non-English languages. This is advantageous for several reasons, namely lower latency (no need to translate twice), and reduced error cascades (e.g., avoiding losing gender and formality information when translating through English).On the downside, adding more languages reduces model capacity per language, which is usually countered by increasing the overall model size, making training harder and inference slower. In this work, we introduce Language-Specific Transformer Layers (LSLs), which allow us to increase model capacity, while keeping the amount of computation and the number of parameters used in the forward pass constant. The key idea is to have some layers of the encoder be source or target language-specific, while keeping the remaining layers shared. We study the best way to place these layers using a neural architecture search inspired approach, and achieve an improvement of 1.3 chrF (1.5 spBLEU) points over not using LSLs on a separate decoder architecture, and 1.9 chrF (2.2 spBLEU) on a shared decoder one.

basic.text_prompt:
The paper "Learning Language-Specific Layers for Multilingual Machine Translation" by Telmo Pessoa Pires and colleagues presents a novel approach to multilingual machine translation (MT) that aims to increase the capacity per language while keeping inference costs constant. The authors introduce Language-Specific Layers (LSLs), which are transformer layers that are selected and trained at inference time based on the source or target language. This allows for a more efficient use of model capacity, as only the relevant sublayer is used during inference.

The paper also explores the optimal placement of LSLs within the model. Instead of manually placing LSLs, the authors let the model learn the best placement by training a large model with shared, source-specific, and target-specific weights for each encoder layer. The placement is determined by the largest weight among the three components. The authors found that this learned architecture outperforms both language adapters and the largest baseline model, with significant improvements for low-resource languages.

The paper reports that the learned architecture achieved better performance across all languages, with statistically significant improvements for 84 out of 90 translation directions. The authors encourage readers to check the full paper for more details and to attend their poster session for further discussions.

formal.text_prompt:
The paper "Learning Language-Specific Layers for Multilingual Machine Translation" by Telmo Pessoa Pires, Robin Schmidt, Yi-Hsiu Liao, and Stephan Peitz, introduces a novel approach to multilingual machine translation (MT) that aims to increase the capacity per language while keeping inference costs constant. The authors propose Language-Specific Layers (LSLs), which are transformer layers that are selected and trained at inference time based on the source or target language. This approach allows for a more efficient use of model capacity, as only the relevant sublayer is activated during inference, thus maintaining constant inference costs.

The paper details the placement of LSLs within the model, with an emphasis on learning the optimal placement through training. The authors found that the source weights in the encoder are generally more important, with the importance shifting towards the target weights as the encoder progresses. The placement of LSLs is determined by the largest weight in each layer, leading to a mixed architecture of shared and language-specific layers.

Experiments were conducted using the WMT21 news translation mask sources for 10 languages, including both high-resource and low-resource languages. The results showed that the learned architecture with LSLs outperformed both the baseline transformer model and the language adapters approach, with significant improvements in chrF, spBLEU, and COMET scores. The improvements were particularly notable for low-resource languages, and the results were statistically significant for 84 out of the 90 translation directions.

The paper concludes that the LSL approach offers a promising direction for enhancing multilingual MT systems, especially in terms of supporting low-resource languages. The authors encourage further exploration of the approach through additional experiments and comparisons with other methods.

detailed.text_prompt:
The paper "Learning Language-Specific Layers for Multilingual Machine Translation" by Telmo Pessoa Pires, Robin Schmidt, Yi-Hsiu Liao, and Stephan Peitz, presents a novel approach to multilingual machine translation. The authors aim to increase the capacity per language in a multilingual model while keeping inference costs constant. They propose the use of Language-Specific Layers (LSLs), which are transformer layers that are trained and selected at inference time based on the source or target language. 

The authors experimented with placing LSLs in the encoder, as placing them in the decoder did not yield significant improvements. They used a large model with shared, source-specific, and target-specific weights to learn the best placement of LSLs. The model was then trained from scratch with the selected architecture. 

The authors trained their model on WMT21 news translation mask sources for 10 languages, including some European languages, some Asian languages, and Swahili as a low-resource language. They evaluated their model on Flores-101 and reported chrF, spBLEU, and COMET scores. Their learned architecture showed significant improvements over both the language adapters approach and the largest baseline model, even though it was faster at inference time. 

The authors found that their approach gave improvements for every language, with large improvements for low-resource languages. They also found that their improvements were statistically significant for 84 out of the 90 translation directions. The paper concludes that the use of LSLs can significantly improve the performance of multilingual machine translation models, especially for low-resource languages.

ref: Sustaining coherent and engaging narratives requires dialogue or storytelling agents to understandhow the personas of speakers or listeners ground the narrative. Specifically, these agents must infer personas of their listeners to produce statements that cater to their interests. They must also learn to maintain consistent speaker personas for themselves throughout the narrative, so that their counterparts feel involved in a realistic conversation or story. However, personas are diverse and complex: they entail large quantities of rich interconnected world knowledge that is challenging to robustly represent in general narrative systems (e.g., a singer is good at singing, and may have attended conservatoire). In this work, we construct a new large-scale persona commonsense knowledge graph, PeaCoK, containing ~100K human-validated persona facts. Our knowledge graph schematizes five dimensions of persona knowledge identified in previous studies of human interactive behaviours, and distils facts in this schema from both existing commonsense knowledge graphs and large-scale pretrained language models. Our analysis indicates that PeaCoK contains rich and precise world persona inferences that help downstream systems generate more consistent and engaging narratives.

basic.text_prompt: The Natural Language Processing Lab at EPFL University, in collaboration with Sony Group Corporation, has developed PeaCoK, a Persona Commonsense Knowledge Graph, to enhance the generation of coherent and engaging narratives. PeaCoK includes 3,800 personas and 40,000 attributes, forming 100,000 personal inferences. The graph was created by selecting personas from existing commonsense graphs, inducing attributes from knowledge graphs and pre-trained language models, and crowdsourcing annotations. PeaCoK was used to train a BART-based common knowledge generator, which outperformed large-scale pre-trained language models in persona attribute inference tasks. The knowledge was also used to improve dialogue generation in a persona-grounded dialogue generation task, resulting in more fluent, consistent, and engaging conversations. The work demonstrates the potential of PeaCoK to train reliable persona knowledge generators and enable more consistent and engaging narrative modeling. The paper and GitHub site are publicly available.

The graph contains rich interconnections between personas, with about 9,200 attributes connected to two or more personas. The graph can help language models learn and generalize persona knowledge, as demonstrated by improved performance in a persona attribute inference task and better dialogue generation in a persona-grounded dialogue generation task.

formal.m_audio_prompt:
PeaCoK is a Persona Commonsense Knowledge Graph developed by the Natural Language Processing Lab at EPFL University in collaboration with Sony Group Corporation. It aims to represent world-level persona knowledge at scale, with about 3,800 personas and 40,000 distinctive attributes, forming approximately 100,000 personal inferences or facts. The graph also includes about 9,200 attributes connected to two or more personas, highlighting the rich interconnections of personas.

PeaCoK was built in three steps: selecting personas from existing commonsense graphs, inducing attributes of personas from commonsense knowledge graphs and large-scale pre-trained language models, and crowdsourcing the annotations of PeaCoK relations using a joint human-AI majority voting scheme. The graph achieved an average accuracy of 87% in F1 for relation annotations, with AI mediating disagreements between human annotators.

The graph can be used to train language models, such as BART, to learn persona knowledge generation capabilities comparable to large-scale language models. In a persona attribute inference task, Comet-BART trained on PeaCoK outperformed large-scale pre-trained language models, achieving better automatic evaluation results and higher accept rates in human evaluation.

PeaCoK can also be used to improve downstream narrative modeling, as demonstrated in a persona-grounded dialogue generation task on the ConvAI2 PersonaChat dataset. The knowledge linker retrieves facts from PeaCoK relevant to each speaker's original persona profile and utterances, converting the retrieved facts into natural language statements to augment each speaker's profile. The P²Bot model, which uses PeaCoK knowledge, achieved better dialogue generation on various aspects, including fluency, consistency, engagement, and persona expression, compared to the Atomic2020 knowledge graph.

In summary, PeaCoK is a world-level Persona Commonsense Knowledge Graph that can be used to train reliable persona knowledge generators and enable more consistent and engaging narrative modeling. The paper and GitHub site for this work are now public, and can be found on the lab's website.

detailed.text_prompt:
The text introduces the work of the Natural Language Processing Lab at EPFL University, Silin, and Sony Group Corporation on "PeaCoK: Persona Commonsense Knowledge for Consistent and Engaging Narratives". The work aims to improve the understanding of personas in narratives, which is crucial for creating coherent and engaging dialogues or stories. The team proposes a Persona-grounded Commonsense Knowledge Graph (PeaCoK) to represent world-level persona knowledge at scale, containing about 3,800 personas and 40,000 distinctive attributes, forming about 100,000 personal inferences or facts. The graph also has about 9,200 attributes connected to two or more personas, contributing to the rich interconnections of personas in PeaCoK.

The team built PeaCoK in three steps: selecting personas from existing commonsense graphs, inducing attributes of personas from commonsense knowledge graphs and large-scale pre-trained language models, and crowdsourcing the annotations of PeaCoK relations using a joint human-AI majority voting scheme. The majority voting with AI in the loop yielded high-quality relation annotations with an average 87% accuracy in F1.

The team used PeaCoK to train a BART-based common knowledge generator on a persona attribute inference task, where the model needs to predict an attribute of a given persona with a target relation. The Comet-BART trained on PeaCoK achieved better automatic evaluation results and a higher accept rate in human evaluation compared to large-scale pre-trained language models, including five-shot GPT-3 and zero-shot GPT-3.5.

The team also explored whether PeaCoK knowledge can be used to improve downstream narrative modeling. They investigated a persona-grounded dialogue generation task on the ConvAI2 PersonaChat data set, using a knowledge linker to retrieve facts from PeaCoK that are relevant to each speaker's original persona profile and utterances. The augmented models achieved better dialogue generation on various aspects, including fluency, consistency, engagement, and persona expression, compared to the baseline dialogue system, P²Bot.

The team also found that PeaCoK's persona-centric commonsense knowledge yielded a more positive impact compared to general social commonsense knowledge. They also stratified their human evaluation results based on the overlap of the two speakers’ augmented PeaCoK knowledge, finding that the winning rates of PeaCoK augmented model increased as the number of shared common attributes between two speakers became larger.

In summary, the team proposed a world-level Persona Commonsense Knowledge Graph (PeaCoK) that contains large-scale, high-quality persona inferences. The knowledge resource can be used to train reliable persona knowledge generators and also enable more consistent and engaging narrative modeling. The paper and GitHub site for this work are public, and can also be found on the lab's website.

ref: Text simplification is an intralingual translation task in which documents, or sentences of a complex source text are simplified for a target audience. The success of automatic text simplification systems is highly dependent on the quality of parallel data used for training and evaluation. To advance sentence simplification and document simplification in German, this paper presents DEplain, a new dataset of parallel, professionally written and manually aligned simplifications in plain German “plain DE” or in German: “Einfache Sprache”. DEplain consists of a news-domain (approx. 500 document pairs, approx. 13k sentence pairs) and a web-domain corpus (approx. 150 aligned documents, approx. 2k aligned sentence pairs). In addition, we are building a web harvester and experimenting with automatic alignment methods to facilitate the integration of non-aligned and to be-published parallel documents. Using this approach, we are dynamically increasing the web-domain corpus, so it is currently extended to approx. 750 document pairs and approx. 3.5k aligned sentence pairs. We show that using DEplain to train a transformer-based seq2seq text simplification model can achieve promising results. We make available the corpus, the adapted alignment methods for German, the web harvester and the trained models here: https://github.com/rstodden/DEPlain.

basic.text_prompt: DEPLAIN is a new corpus for German text identification at both the document and sentence levels, aimed at improving text comprehension for specific target groups. The corpus, created by Regina Stodden, consists of two subcorpora: DEPLAIN-apa, based on news texts, and DEPLAIN-web, covering various domains. It contains 30,450 manually and automatically aligned sentence pairs, with a focus on different simplification techniques. The corpus was used to evaluate automatic alignment methods and to fine-tune language models for automatic text simplification, with the best method being MASSalign. The results serve as a benchmark for future research in the field.

DEPLAIN has been used to evaluate automatic alignment methods, with MASSalign being the best method for German text simplification. Additionally, DEPLAIN has been utilized to fine-tune language models for automatic text simplification, achieving results comparable to or better than baseline scores.

formal.text_prompt:
DEPLAIN is a new corpus for German text identification at both the document and sentence levels, introduced by Regina Stodden. Text simplification is the process of adapting a text to improve comprehension for specific target groups, such as people with reading difficulties or non-native speakers. DEPLAIN addresses issues with existing corpora, which are either too small or have error-prone automatic alignments. The DEPLAIN corpus is divided into two subcorpora: DEPLAIN-apa, based on news texts with 13,000 parallel sentence pairs, and DEPLAIN-web, with 30,450 sentence pairs from various domains. The corpus was manually aligned and analyzed for simplification types, showing a high variety of simplification transformations.

Omar discusses two use cases for DEPLAIN. The first is evaluating automatic alignment methods by using DEPLAIN as a gold standard for sentence alignments in parallel documents of different complexity levels. The second use case involves fine-tuning language models for automatic text simplification. Two models, long-mBART and base mBART, were fine-tuned, showing that basic fine-tuning can achieve scores better than baseline scores, serving as a benchmark for future automatic text simplification research.
formal.f_audio_prompt:
DEPLAIN Corpus: A New Resource for German Text Simplification

Introduction:
- DEPLAIN is a new corpus designed for German text identification at both document and sentence levels.
- Developed by Regina Stodden and Omar, it aims to address the limitations of existing corpora.

Definition of Text Simplification:
- Text simplification is the process of adapting a text to improve comprehension for specific target groups, such as people with reading difficulties or non-native speakers.
- Training a text simplification model requires parallel pairs of texts, such as documents or sentences.

Proposed Corpus DEPLAIN:
- DEPLAIN addresses the issues of small corpus size and error-prone automatic alignments in existing corpora.
- It is split into two subcorpora: DEPLAIN-apa (news texts) and DEPLAIN-web (varied domains).
- DEPLAIN-apa contains 483 documents with approximately 13,000 parallel sentence pairs.
- DEPLAIN-web includes 750 documents with around 30,450 sentence pairs.

Analysis of Sentence Pairs:
- The Bible texts are more strongly simplified than news texts or language learner texts.
- DEPLAIN corpus exhibits a high variety of simplification transformations, including lexical substitution, clause deletion, reordering, and word insertion.

Use Cases for DEPLAIN Corpus:
1. Evaluation of Automatic Alignment Methods:
- DEPLAIN corpus can be used to evaluate automatic alignment methods for German text simplification.
- The best method found was MASSalign, which can be accessed through the paper's code.

2. Automatic Text Simplification by Fine-Tuning Language Models:
- DEPLAIN corpus can be used to fine-tune language models for document-level and sentence-level simplifications.
- The results of these fine-tuning experiments can be found in the paper, and they serve as a benchmark for future research.

Conclusion:
- DEPLAIN corpus is a valuable resource for German text simplification, addressing the limitations of existing corpora.
- It can be used to evaluate automatic alignment methods and fine-tune language models for text simplification.
- The paper provides detailed information on the corpus, analysis, and use cases, as well as the code for running experiments.
formal.m_audio_prompt:
DEPLAIN Corpus: A New Resource for German Text Simplification
DEPLAIN is a newly proposed corpus designed to improve text simplification models for German texts, both at the document and sentence levels. The corpus addresses the limitations of existing corpora, which are either too small or rely on automatic alignment methods that can be error-prone. DEPLAIN is divided into two subcorpora: DEPLAIN-apa and DEPLAIN-web. DEPLAIN-apa is based on news texts and consists of 483 manually aligned documents, resulting in approximately 13,000 parallel sentence pairs. DEPLAIN-web includes various domains and is aligned using both manual and automatic methods, resulting in 30,450 sentence pairs. The DEPLAIN corpus demonstrates a high variety of simplification transformations, including lexical substitution, clause deletion, reordering, and insertion of words. The corpus has been used to evaluate automatic alignment methods and fine-tune language models for text simplification. The best method for automatic alignment in German text simplification is MASSalign, and the code for this method is available in the paper. DEPLAIN has also been used to fine-tune models like long-mBART and mBART for document-level and sentence-level simplification, respectively. The results of these experiments serve as a benchmark for future research in automatic text simplification.

detailed.f_audio_prompt: Hello! Welcome to our presentation of DEPLAIN, a new corpus for German text identification on the document level, and on the sentence level. My name is Regina Stodden, and I will guide you through the first part of the presentation. Let's first define text simplification. Text simplification is a process of adapting a text to improve the text comprehension of it for a specific target group, as people with reading problems or non-native speakers. To train a text simplification model we require parallel pairs of text, for example of documents or sentences. And the example here, you can see a parallel aligned sentence pair of a complex German sentence and its translation into plain language. To simplify the sentence, different techniques are possible as you can see in the example, such as lexical substitution, clause deletion, reordering, or insertion of words. We now propose our new corpus, DEPLAIN because in the recent years, there were some problems with existing corpora. So for example, these corpora here are too small to train a text simplification model on. The other three models which are proposed in recent years are all automatically aligned, which means they can be error-prone in their alignments. Therefore, we propose our new corpus DEPLAIN, which is split into two subcorpora: DEPLAIN-apa and DEPLAIN-web. DEPLAIN-apa is based on news texts. In DEPLAIN-apa, we aligned 483 documents all manually. It results in roughly 13,000 parallel sentence pairs. For DEPLAIN-web, this corpus includes different domains and we also align all of these 750 documents, on the one hand manually and on the other hand with automatic alignment methods. In total we result in 30,450 sentence pairs. We analyzed our sentence pairs a little bit more, so for example, on the type of simplification. As you can see here, the Bible texts are much, stronger simplified than for example the news text, or the language learner texts. On all levels, regarding for example lexical simplification, structure simplification, also overall level of simplification. Furthermore, you can see that our DEPLAIN corpus has a high variety of different simplification transformations. So for example, in the DEPLAIN-apa corpus we have much more reorderings and word additions than we have in the DEPLAIN-web corpus.
