detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Title: Exploring Large Language Models for Classical Philology

Introduction:
Frederick Riemenschneider presents a talk on the intersection of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and classical philology, focusing on the development of language models for Ancient Greek and Latin. The presentation, titled "Exploring Large Language Models for Classical Philology," aims to introduce valuable resources for these classical languages and explore the implications and challenges of multilinguality in language models.

Current Landscape of Language Models:
The talk highlights the recent advancements in language models, including Latin BERT (2020), Ancient Greek BERT (2021), and GreTa (2022). These models are encoder-only BERT models, all monolingual, and are not pre-trained on Ancient Greek texts. The performance of these models is uncharted territory, and there is a lack of robust evaluation to understand their capabilities and areas for improvement.

Goals of the Project:
The project aims to make existing models comparable, push the state-of-the-art further, explore different model architectures, and introduce multilingual models. The project has developed two monolingual models for Ancient Greek, GreBERTa and GreTa, and two multilingual models, PhilBERTa and PhilTa, pre-trained on Ancient Greek, Latin, and English data.

Pre-training Data:
The project gathered pre-training data for Ancient Greek, leveraging the Internet Archive and developing a new pre-training corpus. For the multilingual models, additional resources were used, including the Corpus Corporum for Latin and English texts related to antiquity.

Benchmarking:
The models were benchmarked using the Universal Dependencies treebanks for Greek and the EvaLatina 2022 dataset for Latin. The focus was on three main tasks: part-of-speech tagging, dependency parsing, and lemmatization. The models outperformed the current state-of-the-art for both Ancient Greek and Latin.

Analysis of GreTa:
The talk analyzes the behavior of GreTa's encoder, showing that the performance of GreTa's encoder is extremely bad after one epoch, but improves with more training. The encoder-decoder models excel in lemmatization, achieving a 5 percentage point improvement over the existing state-of-the-art for Ancient Greek.

Semantic and World Knowledge Capabilities:
The models were tested for their semantic and world knowledge capabilities, such as distinguishing synonyms from antonyms and identifying relations between heroes and gods. The multilingual models did not show significant performance gains over the monolingual models.

Conclusion:
The presentation concludes that the project has introduced new powerful language models for classical philology, initialized from scratch and using a native tokenizer. The models pre-trained both encoder-only and encoder-decoder architectures as well as multilingual models, allowing Latin and Greek text to be processed by the same model. The high-quality pre-training dataset for ancient Greek and the rigorous benchmarking of previous and own models were also discussed.

The models were pre-trained using a new high-quality corpus from the Internet Archive and benchmarked against existing models. ref: Natural language often contains ambiguities that can lead to misinterpretation and miscommunication. While humans can handle ambiguities effectively by asking clarifying questions and/or relying on contextual cues and common-sense knowledge, resolving ambiguities can be notoriously hard for machines. In this work, we study ambiguities that arise in text-to-image generative models. We curate the Text-to-image Ambiguity Benchmark (TAB) dataset to study different types of ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. We then propose the Text-to-ImagE Disambiguation (TIED) framework to disambiguate the prompts given to the text-to-image generative models by soliciting clarifications from the end user. Through automatic and human evaluations, we show the effectiveness of our framework in generating more faithful images aligned with end user intention in the presence of ambiguities.

basic.text_prompt: Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presented their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The team focused on the challenges posed by ambiguous prompts, which can lead to varied interpretations and difficulty in generating faithful images. They developed a pipeline that includes curating a benchmark dataset with different types of ambiguities, a prompt disambiguation framework that uses in-context learning to generate clarifying questions or visual setups, and an evaluation framework that uses a Visual Question Answering (VQA) model to assess the faithfulness of generated images to user intentions. The findings showed that their framework effectively mitigates ambiguities and that the evaluation framework aligns with human judgment. The work aims to improve the reliability and accuracy of text-to-image models in interpreting and fulfilling user requests.

The speaker describes a pipeline that includes curating a benchmark dataset, using a prompt disambiguation framework, and evaluating the generated images with a VQA model. The findings show that disambiguation has a positive effect on faithful generation, and the automatic evaluation framework aligns with human evaluation.

Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presented their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The team focused on the challenges posed by ambiguous prompts, which can lead to varied interpretations and hinder the generation of faithful images. To address this, they developed a pipeline that includes curating a benchmark dataset with different types of ambiguities, a prompt disambiguation framework, and an evaluation framework to assess the faithfulness of generated images to user intentions.

The benchmark dataset, a modified version of LAVA, was used to train a language model to generate clarifying questions or different visual interpretations. The user's responses helped disambiguate the prompts. The disambiguated prompts were then used to generate images, which were evaluated using a Visual Question Answering (VQA) model to determine if they matched the user's intention.

Findings from the study indicated that the disambiguation process had a positive effect on the faithfulness of image generation. The automatic evaluation framework showed agreement with human evaluations, suggesting its reliability. The paper also discussed the disparity in resolving different types of ambiguities and provided additional insights and discussions.

In conclusion, Mehrabi's team has made significant strides in understanding and mitigating ambiguities in text-to-image models, contributing to the development of more accurate and user-intention-aligned generative AI systems.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: Resolving Ambiguities in Text-to-Image Generative Models

Introduction:
Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presents their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The research aims to address the challenges posed by ambiguous prompts and propose frameworks to mitigate these ambiguities, as well as evaluate the faithfulness of generated images to user intention.

Problem Statement:
Text-to-image models often struggle to generate accurate images when provided with ambiguous prompts. Ambiguities can arise from various sources, such as multiple interpretations of a prompt or unclear visual setups. These ambiguities make it difficult for the models to generate images that align with the user's intended meaning.

Research Objectives:
The primary objectives of this research are to:
1. Curate a benchmark dataset that covers different types of ambiguities in text-to-image prompts.
2. Develop a prompt disambiguation framework that can gather external signals to disambiguate the prompt, either by asking clarifying questions or generating different possible visual setups.
3. Propose an automatic evaluation framework to assess the faithfulness of generated images to user intention.

Methodology:
The research team followed a pipeline to address the problem statement:
1. Curated a benchmark dataset by modifying an existing corpus called LAVA, which covers different types of ambiguities.
2. Developed a prompt disambiguation framework that uses in-context learning to generate clarifying questions or different possible visual setups, depending on the ambiguity type.
3. Proposed an automatic evaluation framework that uses a VQA (Visual Question Answering) model to evaluate the faithfulness of generated images to user intention.

Findings:
The research team found that there is disparity in resolving ambiguities for different ambiguity types. However, disambiguation using their framework has a positive effect on faithful generation. The automatic evaluation framework is in agreement with human evaluation, making it a reliable tool for evaluating text-to-image models.

Conclusion:
In conclusion, the research presented by Ninareh Mehrabi and her team addresses the challenges posed by ambiguities in text-to-image generative models.

In this presentation, Ninareh Mehrabi discusses the work done by Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The team aimed to study existing ambiguities in prompts provided to these models and propose frameworks to mitigate such ambiguities, as well as frameworks to evaluate whether the generated images are faithful to user intention.

The team curated a benchmark dataset covering different types of ambiguities and developed a pipeline that includes a prompt disambiguation framework. This framework uses language models to generate clarifying questions or different possible visual interpretations, which are then used to disambiguate the original ambiguous prompts. The disambiguated prompts are then input into text-to-image models, and the generated images are evaluated using an automatic evaluation framework to determine if they are faithful to user intention.

The findings of the work show that there is disparity in resolving ambiguities for different ambiguity types, but overall, disambiguation using the proposed framework has a positive effect on faithful generation. The automatic evaluation framework is in agreement with human evaluation and can be used reliably to evaluate text-to-image models.

In conclusion, the work presented by Ninareh Mehrabi and her team addresses the issue of ambiguities in text-to-image generative models and proposes solutions to mitigate and evaluate these ambiguities, ultimately improving the faithfulness of generated images to user intention.

detailed.text_prompt:
Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presented their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The team focused on the challenges posed by ambiguous prompts, which can lead to multiple interpretations and hinder the generation of faithful images. To address this, they developed a pipeline that includes a benchmark dataset, a prompt disambiguation framework, and an evaluation framework.

The benchmark dataset, derived from the LAVA corpus, includes various types of ambiguities. The disambiguation framework uses a language model to generate clarifying questions or different visual interpretations, which are then answered by the user to disambiguate the original prompt. The final disambiguated prompt is used to generate images with a text-to-image model.

The evaluation framework assesses whether the generated images align with the user's intention. It uses a Visual Question Answering (VQA) model to determine if the images satisfy the user's intent, as expressed in a question format. The team's findings indicate that their disambiguation framework generally improves the faithfulness of image generation. Additionally, the automatic evaluation framework aligns with human evaluation, making it a reliable tool for assessing text-to-image models.

In conclusion, Mehrabi's team has made significant strides in understanding and resolving ambiguities in text-to-image models, with the potential to enhance the accuracy and reliability of these systems.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
In this talk, Ninareh Mehrabi presents their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The goal of their research is to study existing ambiguities in prompts provided to these models and propose frameworks to mitigate such ambiguities as well as evaluate whether the generated images are faithful to user intention.

The first step in their work is to curate a benchmark dataset that covers different types of ambiguities. They use a modified version of the LAVA corpus, which includes various ambiguous prompts. The language model in their framework generates clarifying questions or different possible visual interpretations to disambiguate the prompts. The user then provides an answer based on their intended meaning, and the disambiguated prompt is obtained by concatenating the signal to the original ambiguous prompt.

Once the prompts are disambiguated, the team evaluates whether the generated images are faithful to user intention. They propose an automatic evaluation framework that uses a VQA model to input the images and the human's intention in question format. The VQA model then evaluates whether the image satisfies the user's intention or not.

The findings of their research show that there is disparity in resolving ambiguities for different ambiguity types. However, disambiguation using their framework has a positive effect on faithful generation. Additionally, their automatic evaluation framework is in agreement with human evaluation, making it a reliable tool for evaluating text-to-image models.

In conclusion, the work presented by Ninareh Mehrabi and their team focuses on studying ambiguities in text-to-image models, proposing frameworks to mitigate and evaluate ambiguities, and demonstrating the effectiveness of their approach in generating faithful images.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Title: Resolving Ambiguities in Text-to-Image Generative Models

Summary:
Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presented their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. The research focused on studying existing ambiguities in prompts provided to these models and proposing frameworks to mitigate such ambiguities, as well as frameworks to evaluate whether the generated images are faithful to user intention.

The team curated a benchmark dataset covering different types of ambiguities, which was used to test a prompt disambiguation framework. This framework aimed to gather external signals to disambiguate the prompt, either by asking clarifying questions from the user or generating different possible visual setups. The disambiguated prompts were then input into a text-to-image model, and the generated images were evaluated to determine if they were faithful to user intention.

The evaluation framework used a VQA (Visual Question Answering) model to assess whether the generated images satisfied the user's intention. The results showed that there was disparity in resolving ambiguities for different ambiguity types, but overall, disambiguation using the proposed framework had a positive effect on faithful generation. The automatic evaluation framework was found to be in agreement with human evaluation, making it a reliable tool for evaluating text-to-image models.

In conclusion, the research presented by Ninareh Mehrabi and her team demonstrated the importance of resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. By proposing frameworks to mitigate ambiguities and evaluate generated images, the team contributed to improving the faithfulness of these models to user intention.

short.text_prompt: Ninareh Mehrabi, a postdoctoral scientist at Amazon Alexa AI's Responsible AI team, presented their work on resolving ambiguities in text-to-image generative models. They developed a framework to disambiguate these prompts by asking clarifying questions or generating different visual interpretations. A benchmark dataset was created to cover various ambiguity types. The disambiguated prompts were then evaluated using a text-to-image model and a Visual Question Answering (VQA) model to determine if the generated images matched the user's intention. The findings showed that disambiguation improved the faithfulness of image generation, and the evaluation framework aligned with human judgment. The work aims to enhance the accuracy and reliability of text-to-image models in interpreting user prompts.

We curate a benchmark dataset covering different types of ambiguities and propose frameworks to both mitigate and evaluate ambiguities provided to text-to-image models. Our approach involves disambiguating prompts using a language model that generates clarifying questions or different possible visual interpretations. We then evaluate the generated images using an automatic evaluation framework that compares the original ambiguous prompt with the disambiguated one, and assesses whether the generated images are faithful to user intention. Our findings show that disambiguation has a positive effect on faithful generation, and our evaluation framework is reliable and in agreement with human evaluation.

ref: Dialogue, the most fundamental and specially privileged arena of language, gains increasing ubiquity across the Web in recent years. Quickly going through the long dialogue context and capturing salient information scattered over the whole dialogue session benefit users in many real-world Web applications such as email thread summarization and meeting minutes draft. Dialogue summarization is a challenging task in that dialogue has dynamic interaction nature and presumably inconsistent information flow among various speakers. Many researchers address this task by modeling dialogue with pre-computed static graph structure using external linguistic toolkits. However, such methods heavily depend on the reliability of external tools and the static graph construction is disjoint with the graph representation learning phase, which makes the graph can’t be dynamically adapted for the downstream summarization task. In this paper, we propose a Static-Dynamic graph-based Dialogue Summarization model (SDDS), which fuses prior knowledge from human expertise and adaptively learns the graph structure in an end-to-end learning fashion. To verify the effectiveness of SDDS, we conduct experiments on three benchmark datasets (SAMSum, MediaSum, and DialogSum) and the results verify the superiority of SDDS.

basic.text_prompt: Shen Gao and colleagues from Shandong University presented their work on "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph," a method to distill key information from dialogues. Existing methods rely on pre-computed static graph structures, which can be error-prone and inflexible. Their SDDS model addresses this by combining static and dynamic graph representations, using an Utterance Encoder, heuristic methods for static graph construction, a Dynamic Graph module for semantic relationships, and a pre-trained language model for summary generation. The model aims to improve dialogue summarization by integrating both static and dynamic graph information, and the code and data are available on GitHub.
basic.f_audio_prompt: The text introduces the "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph" model, which aims to distill salient information from dialogues into concise summaries. The model addresses the challenges of dialogue summarization by combining static and dynamic graph structures to capture dialogue relationships. The SDDS model consists of four main components: Utterance Encoder, Static Graph, Static-Dynamic Graph module, and Summary Generator. The model uses various methods to build dialogue structures, including discourse parsing, speaker interaction frequency, and utterance position graphs. The dynamic graph module employs multi-head attention to calculate semantic relationships, and the fusion method combines the static and dynamic graph representations. The model's code and data are available on GitHub.
basic.m_audio_prompt: Dialogue summarization aims to condense important information from a dialogue into a brief summary, which is useful for quickly understanding the main points of a conversation involving multiple participants. The SDDS model addresses the challenges of dialogue summarization by combining static and dynamic graph structures. It uses an utterance encoder, static graph construction, a Static-Dynamic Graph module, and a pre-trained language model to generate summaries. The model also incorporates speaker relationship modeling, position information, and cross-graph fusion to improve the accuracy of the summaries.

formal.text_prompt: Shen Gao and colleagues from Shandong University present their work on "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph," a method to distill key information from dialogues. The SDDS model they propose addresses the limitations of existing methods that rely on pre-computed static graph structures, which can be error-prone and inflexible. The SDDS model consists of four main components: Utterance Encoder, Static-Dynamic Graph module, and a pre-trained language model as the Summary Generator. The model captures both static dialogue structure using heuristic methods and dynamic semantic relationships using a multi-head attention model. The fusion of static and dynamic structures is achieved through a unified graph and a dual cross-attention mechanism. The team has made their code and data available on GitHub.
formal.f_audio_prompt: The SDDS model for dialogue summarization combines static and dynamic graph structures to create a concise and accurate summary. It uses an utterance encoder, static graph construction, a Static-Dynamic Graph module, and a pre-trained language model as the Summary Generator. The model addresses the drawbacks of existing methods by capturing semantic relationships between utterances and integrating static and dynamic graph representations.
formal.m_audio_prompt: The SDDS model for dialogue summarization combines static and dynamic graph structures to create a concise and accurate summary. It uses an utterance encoder, static graph construction, a Static-Dynamic Graph module, and a pre-trained language model as the Summary Generator. The model captures the dialogue structure information through four heuristic dialogue structure modeling methods, including discourse parsing graph, speaker relationship modeling, utterance position graph, and cross-graph fusion. The dynamic graph module uses multi-head attention to calculate semantic relationships between utterances. The fusion method combines the relation matrix of the dynamic graph and the adjacent matrix of the static graph into a unified graph. The model's graph representation is incorporated into the generation process using a dual cross-attention mechanism. The code and data for the SDDS model are available on GitHub.

informal.text_prompt:
The work "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph" by Shen Gao and colleagues from Shandong University focuses on creating concise summaries of dialogues by integrating both static and dynamic graph structures. Dialogue summarization is challenging due to the need to distill key information from complex, multi-participant conversations. Existing methods rely on pre-computed static graph structures, which have two main drawbacks: dependency on external linguistic tools and a lack of adaptability to the summarization task.

The proposed SDDS model addresses these issues with four main components:
1. Utterance Encoder: Converts dialogue utterances into vector representations.
2. Static Graph Construction: Uses heuristic methods to build a static graph representing the dialogue structure.
3. Static-Dynamic Graph Module: Combines multiple static graphs and uses a dynamic graph module to capture semantic relationships between utterances based on their vector representations.
4. Summary Generator: Employs a pre-trained language model to fuse the static and dynamic structures into a final summary.

The static graph construction involves:
- Discourse Parsing Graph: Uses discourse parsing tools to create a dependency-based dialogue structure.
- Speaker Relationship Modeling: Analyzes speaker interactions using a sliding window approach.
- Position Information: Utilizes relative distances between utterances as edge features.

The dynamic graph module employs a multi-head attention model to determine semantic relationships between utterances without relying on pre-computed structures. The fusion method combines the static and dynamic graphs into a unified graph, which is then integrated into the summarization process using a dual cross-attention mechanism.

The team has made the code and data available on GitHub for further exploration.
informal.f_audio_prompt: Dialogue summarization is a challenging task in the field of text summarization, aiming to distill the key information from a dialogue context into a concise summary. This can help people quickly grasp the main ideas of a conversation without having to go through the entire dialogue. However, existing methods for dialogue summarization often rely on pre-computed static graph structures, which have two main drawbacks: they depend on the accuracy of external linguistic tools, and they do not adapt well to the downstream summarization task. To address these issues, we propose the SDDS model, which consists of four main components: an Utterance Encoder, a Static Graph module, a Dynamic Graph module, and a Summary Generator. The Utterance Encoder encodes the dialogue context into vector representations, while the Static Graph module combines multiple static graphs to capture the semantic relationship between utterances. The Dynamic Graph module uses a multi-head attention model to calculate the relationship between utterances based on their deep vector representation. Finally, the Summary Generator fuses the static dialogue structure and the dynamically learned dialogue structure into the final summary. Our model also includes a speaker relationship modeling method and a position information graph to capture the fine-grained interaction between speakers and the position of utterances. We have released the code and data on GitHub for others to use and improve upon.
informal.m_audio_prompt: The SDDS model for dialogue summarization aims to capture both static and dynamic structures in a dialogue context. It uses an Utterance Encoder, static graph construction, a Static-Dynamic Graph module, and a Summary Generator to create a concise summary of the dialogue. The model addresses the drawbacks of existing methods by using a multi-head attention model for dynamic graph construction and a fusion method to combine static and dynamic graphs. The code and data are available on GitHub.

detailed.text_prompt:
The work "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph" by Shen Gao and colleagues from Shandong University presents a novel approach to dialogue summarization, a task that involves condensing the key information from a dialogue into a brief summary. The team identifies two main issues with existing methods: reliance on external linguistic tools that may not always be accurate, and the static nature of graph structures that cannot adapt to the specific needs of the summarization task.

To address these issues, the SDDS (Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph) model is proposed, consisting of four main components. The first component is an Utterance Encoder, which transforms dialogue utterances into vector representations. The second component is a Static Graph module, which uses heuristic methods to model the dialogue structure, including discourse parsing, speaker interaction, and position information. The third component is a Dynamic Graph module, which employs a multi-head attention model to capture the semantic relationships between utterances without relying on pre-computed structures. The final component is a Summary Generator, which uses a pre-trained language model to integrate the static and dynamic graph information into the final summary.

The team also introduces a fusion method to combine the relation matrix from the dynamic graph and the adjacent matrix from the static graph into a unified graph. This unified graph is then used in a dual cross-attention mechanism, which incorporates the graph representation into the generation process.

The SDDS model aims to overcome the limitations of existing dialogue summarization methods by combining the strengths of both static and dynamic graph structures. The team has made the code and data available on GitHub for further research and development.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
The work "Dialogue Summarization with Static-Dynamic Structure Fusion Graph" by Shen Gao and colleagues aims to address the challenge of summarizing dialogues, which is a complex task in text summarization research. The goal is to create concise summaries that capture the main ideas of each participant in a dialogue without having to review the entire context. Existing methods rely on pre-computed static graph structures using external linguistic tools, but these have limitations such as dependency on external tools and disjointed graph representations.

The proposed SDDS model consists of four main components: Utterance Encoder, Static Graph, Static-Dynamic Graph module, and Summary Generator. The Utterance Encoder encodes the dialogue context into vector representations. The Static Graph is constructed using a data structure modeling method, while the Static-Dynamic Graph module combines multiple static graphs and captures semantic relationships between utterances using a dynamic graph module. The Summary Generator fuses the static dialogue structure and the dynamically learned dialogue structure into the final summary.

The model employs four heuristic dialogue structure modeling methods to build relationships between utterances using a graph network. These methods include Discourse Parsing Graph, Key Co-occurrence, Speaker Relationship Modeling, and Utterance Position Graph. The Dynamic Graph module uses multi-head attention to calculate semantic relationships between utterances without relying on pre-computed or heuristic methods. The fusion method combines the relation matrix of the dynamic graph and the adjacent matrix of the static graph into a unified graph, which is then integrated into the generation process using a dual cross-attention mechanism.

The code and data for this work have been released on GitHub, and a QR code is provided for easy access.

ref: Recent works show the effectiveness of cache-based neural coreference resolution models on long documents. These models incrementally process a long document from left to right and extract relations between mentions and entities in a cache, resulting in much lower memory and computation cost compared to computing all mentions in parallel. However, they do not handle cache misses when high-quality entities are purged from the cache, which causes wrong assignments and leads to prediction errors. We propose a new hybrid cache that integrates two eviction policies to capture global and local entities separately, and effectively reduces the aggregated cache misses up to half as before, while improving F1 score of coreference by 0.7 5.7pt. As such, the hybrid policy can accelerate existing cache-based models and offer a new long document coreference resolution solution. Results show that our method outperforms existing methods on four benchmarks while saving up to 83% of inference time against non-cache-based models. Further, we achieve a new state-of-the-art on a long document coreference benchmark, LitBank.

basic.text_prompt: Qipeng Guo from AWS presented the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution" work, focusing on the task of coreference resolution, which involves identifying and clustering mentions of entities across a text. Traditional methods have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods have reduced this to linear. However, these methods struggle with long documents where topics change, leading to high cache misses. The proposed dual cache system, which includes both a local cache (using LRU eviction) and a global cache (using LFU eviction), addresses this by storing local and global entities separately. Evaluations on public benchmarks and a book-level document show that dual cache outperforms single cache methods, significantly reducing cache misses and offering the highest performance/cost ratio.
basic.f_audio_prompt: The talk introduced the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution" method, which uses a local and global cache to store local and global entities separately. This approach outperforms single cache methods by reducing cache misses and is more cost-effective. The method is evaluated on four benchmarks, showing better performance with training data and a significant reduction in cache misses for book-level documents.
basic.m_audio_prompt: The talk introduced the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution" method, which uses a local cache and a global cache to store local and global entities, respectively. This approach outperforms single cache methods by reducing cache misses and is more cost-effective.

formal.text_prompt: Qipeng Guo from AWS presented the work "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution". Coreference resolution is the task of identifying and clustering mentions of entities in a document. Traditional methods for this task have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods have reduced this to a linear level. However, these methods often lead to high cache misses in long documents due to the LRU eviction policy. Guo's team proposed a dual cache that uses both a local cache and a global cache, with the local cache using an LRU eviction policy and the global cache using an LFU eviction policy. The dual cache was evaluated on four public benchmarks and a book-level document, showing that it outperforms single cache methods and significantly reduces cache misses. The team concluded that the dual cache is the most cost-effective method for coreference resolution.
formal.f_audio_prompt: The text introduces the work of Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution, which aims to improve the efficiency of coreference resolution in long documents. Coreference resolution involves identifying and clustering mentions of entities across a text. Traditional methods have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods reduce it to linear. However, these methods struggle with long documents due to frequent topic switches. The proposed solution is a dual cache system with a local cache and a global cache, using LRU and LFU eviction policies, respectively. This system outperforms single cache methods, reduces cache misses, and is cost-effective.

informal.text_prompt:
Qipeng Guo from AWS presented the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution" work, which addresses the task of coreference resolution in documents. Coreference resolution involves identifying and clustering mentions of entities across a text. Traditional methods have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods have reduced this to linear complexity. However, these methods often face high cache misses in long documents due to the LRU eviction policy.

The proposed solution, the dual cache, consists of a local cache and a global cache. The local cache uses an LRU eviction policy for local entities, while the global cache uses an LFU policy for global entities. This dual cache system classifies new mentions as either local or global entities and stores them accordingly. The model scans the document from left to right, adding new or updated entities to the appropriate cache and evicting entities when the cache is full.

The dual cache was evaluated on four public benchmarks, including LitBank, OntoNotes, and WikiCoref datasets. It outperformed single cache methods, especially in book-level documents, and significantly reduced cache misses. The dual cache also demonstrated a high performance/cost ratio, making it the most cost-effective solution among single cache methods. In conclusion, the dual cache effectively stores local and global entities, reducing cache misses and offering superior performance and cost-efficiency.
informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution

Introduction:
- Qipeng Guo from AWS introduces the task of coreference resolution, which involves identifying and clustering mentions that refer to the same entity in a document.

Problem with Conventional Methods:
- Conventional methods have quadratic complexity in computation and memory consumption, requiring the enumeration of all pairs of mentions.

Cache-Based Methods:
- Recent cache-based methods use a fixed-size cache to reduce complexity to a linear level.
- LRU eviction policy is used, but it leads to high cache misses in long documents due to topic switches.

Proposed Solution: Dual Cache
- Dual cache consists of a local cache and a global cache.
- Local cache stores local entities with LRU eviction policy.
- Global cache stores global entities with LFU policy, evicting the least frequently used entity when full.

Evaluation:
- Dual cache is evaluated on four public benchmarks, including LitBank, OntoNotes, and WikiCoref datasets.
- With training data, dual cache performs better than baselines, even with unbounded memory.
- Without training data, the model with unbounded memory performs slightly better, but dual cache is still faster.
- Performance gap is larger for book-level documents, and dual cache significantly reduces cache misses compared to single cache.

Conclusion:
- Dual cache uses a local and global cache to separately store local and global entities.
- It outperforms single cache methods and largely reduces cache misses.
- Dual cache has the highest performance/cost ratio, making it the most cost-effective method.

Summary:
Qipeng Guo from AWS presents the concept of dual cache for long document neural coreference resolution. The proposed solution addresses the limitations of conventional methods by using a local and global cache to store local and global entities, respectively. The evaluation of dual cache on various benchmarks shows that it outperforms single cache methods, significantly reducing cache misses and offering the highest performance/cost ratio.
informal.m_audio_prompt: Dual cache for long document neural coreference resolution: A novel approach to efficiently handle coreference resolution in long documents by utilizing a local cache and a global cache, resulting in reduced cache misses and improved performance/cost ratio compared to single cache methods.

detailed.text_prompt:
The speaker, Qipeng Guo from AWS, presented a talk on the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution". The task of coreference resolution involves identifying and clustering mentions of entities across a text. Traditional methods for this task have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods have reduced this to a linear level. However, these methods often lead to high cache misses in long documents due to the LRU eviction policy. 

To address this, Guo and his team proposed a dual cache system that uses both a local cache and a global cache. The local cache stores entities with an LRU eviction policy, while the global cache stores entities with an LFU policy. The model scans the document from left to right, classifying new mentions as either new entities or belonging to an entity in the cache. If qualified, the entity is added to the global cache; otherwise, it is added to the local cache. When the cache is full, the eviction policy is triggered. 

The dual cache system was evaluated on four public benchmarks, including LitBank, OntoNotes, and WikiCoref datasets. The results showed that the dual cache system outperformed single cache methods, especially in book-level documents, and significantly reduced cache misses. The dual cache system also demonstrated the highest performance/cost ratio among cache-based models. In conclusion, the dual cache system effectively stores local and global entities, reducing cache misses and offering a cost-effective solution for long document coreference resolution.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
The task of coreference resolution involves identifying and clustering mentions that refer to the same entity within a document. Traditional methods for this task have a quadratic complexity in terms of computation and memory consumption, as they require enumerating all possible pairs of mentions. Cache-based methods have been proposed to reduce this complexity to a linear level by using a fixed-size cache. However, in long documents, the topic may change frequently, causing mentions of an entity to be scattered across a wide range of text. This leads to high cache misses when using the Least Recently Used (LRU) eviction policy.

To address this issue, we propose a dual cache system that consists of a local cache and a global cache. The local cache stores local entities using the LRU eviction policy, while the global cache stores global entities using the Least Frequently Used (LFU) policy. The dual cache works by scanning the document from left to right and classifying each mention as either a new entity or belonging to an entity in the cache. If the mention is qualified, it is added to the appropriate cache. When the cache is full, the eviction policy is triggered to evict an entity.

Our evaluation of the dual cache on four public benchmarks shows that it outperforms single cache methods, especially for book-level documents. The performance gap between the baseline and dual cache is much larger for book-level documents, and dual cache significantly reduces cache misses compared to a single cache. Additionally, dual cache has the highest performance/cost ratio among cache-based models.

In conclusion, the dual cache system uses a local and global cache to separately store local and global entities, outperforming single cache methods and reducing cache misses. It is also the most cost-effective option for cache-based models.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Title: Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution

Introduction:
Qipeng Guo from AWS introduces the concept of coreference resolution, which involves identifying and clustering mentions of entities within a document. The task is challenging due to the quadratic complexity of conventional methods, which require enumerating all pairs of mentions. Cache-based methods have been proposed to reduce this complexity to a linear level, but they face issues with high cache misses in long documents.

Proposed Solution:
The proposed solution is a dual cache system that consists of a local cache and a global cache. The local cache stores entities using an LRU (Least Recently Used) eviction policy, while the global cache stores entities using an LFU (Least Frequently Used) policy. This dual cache system works by scanning the document from left to right, classifying mentions as new or existing entities, and evaluating their frequency. If a mention is qualified, it is added to the appropriate cache. When the cache is full, the eviction policy is triggered to remove an entity.

Evaluation:
The dual cache system was evaluated on four public benchmarks, including LitBank, OntoNotes, and WikiCoref datasets. The results showed that the dual cache outperformed single cache methods, even when using unbounded memory. However, without training data, the model with unbounded memory performed slightly better, but the dual cache still had a faster performance. The performance gap was more significant for book-level documents, and the dual cache significantly reduced cache misses compared to a single cache.

Conclusion:
The dual cache system offers a cost-effective solution for coreference resolution in long documents. It outperforms single cache methods and reduces cache misses, making it the most efficient and effective approach.

short.text_prompt: Qipeng Guo from AWS introduced the "Dual Cache for Long Document Neural Coreference Resolution" method, which addresses the task of coreference resolution by identifying and clustering mentions of entities across a text. Traditional methods have quadratic complexity, but cache-based methods reduce it to linear. However, these methods struggle with long documents where topics switch frequently, leading to high cache misses. The proposed dual cache solution features a local cache with LRU eviction and a global cache with LFU eviction, working together to store local and global entities. Evaluations on public benchmarks and a book-level document show that dual cache outperforms single cache methods, significantly reducing cache misses and offering the highest performance/cost ratio.

ref: Abductive reasoning aims to find plausible explanations for an event. This style of reasoning is critical for commonsense tasks where there are often multiple plausible explanations. Existing approaches for abductive reasoning in natural language processing (NLP) often rely on manually generated annotations for supervision; however, such annotations can be subjective and biased. Instead of using direct supervision, this work proposes an approach for abductive commonsense reasoning that exploits the fact that only a subset of explanations is correct for a given context. The method uses posterior regularization to enforce a mutual exclusion constraint, encouraging the model to learn the distinction between fluent explanations and plausible ones. We evaluate our approach on a diverse set of abductive reasoning datasets; experimental results show that our approach outperforms or is comparable to directly applying pretrained language models in a zero-shot manner and other knowledge-augmented zero-shot methods.

basic.text_prompt: Wenting Zhao, a PhD student at Cornell University, presented a paper titled "Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations." The paper introduces an unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization) for abductive reasoning, which aims to identify plausible explanations without requiring prior knowledge of their plausibility. LiPoR maximizes the marginal likelihood of an outcome given a context by marginalizing over possible explanations, and it includes a regularizer that enforces mutual exclusivity among explanations. The method was tested on the AlphaNLI dataset and outperformed other models, including a zero-shot GPT-3 baseline, by over 4 absolute points in accuracy.

LiPoR treats explanations as latent variables and maximizes the marginal likelihood of the outcome given the context by marginalizing other possible explanations. The method also includes a regularizer that enforces mutual exclusivity among explanations, ensuring that only plausible explanations are preferred. The paper demonstrates that LiPoR outperforms existing approaches, including a strong zero-shot GPT-3 baseline, on the AlphaNLI dataset.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations

Introduction:
In this talk, I present our recent paper titled "Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations." My name is Wenting Zhao, and I am a PhD student at Cornell University. The goal of our research is to explore an unsupervised learning method for abductive reasoning, which is the process of inferring the most plausible explanation for a given context and outcome.

Concrete Example:
To illustrate abductive reasoning, let's consider a concrete example: "Emily was stuck in traffic, and she made it to her flight." In this scenario, we have a context (X) and an outcome (Y). Our task is to identify a plausible explanation that bridges the information gap between the context and the outcome. In this case, two possible explanations are given: "Her flight was delayed" and "Her flight left on time." The goal is to determine which explanation is more plausible.

Formal Definition:
Abductive reasoning can be formally defined as follows: Given a context (X) and an outcome (Y), the goal is to identify a plausible subset of explanations (Z) that can bridge the information gap between X and Y. In our example, Explanation 1 ("Her flight was delayed") is more plausible than Explanation 2 ("Her flight left on time") because it provides the necessary missing information to explain how Emily made it to her flight despite being stuck in traffic.

Current Approaches:
Current approaches to abductive reasoning rely heavily on supervised methods, which require the annotation of plausible explanations. However, this process can be noisy and subjective, as demonstrated by a recent experiment where crowd workers disagreed on 60% of over 1,000 explanations. This raises the question: "Is it possible to learn abductive reasoning without supervision regarding the plausibility of explanations?"

Our Answer:
We believe that it is possible to learn abductive reasoning without supervision. To address this question, we introduce an unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization). LiPoR treats Explanation Z as a latent variable and maximizes the marginal likelihood of the outcome (Y) given the context (X) by marginalizing other possible explanations in Z.
informal.m_audio_prompt: In this presentation, Wenting Zhao introduces a new unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization) for abductive reasoning. The goal of abductive reasoning is to identify plausible explanations that bridge the gap between a given context and an outcome. Current approaches rely on supervised methods, but these require annotated plausible explanations, which can be subjective and noisy. LiPoR addresses this issue by treating explanations as latent variables and maximizing the marginal likelihood of the outcome given the context without needing to know which explanations are plausible. The method also includes a regularizer that enforces mutual exclusivity among explanations, ensuring that only plausible explanations are preferred. On the AlphaNLI dataset, LiPoR outperforms other models, including a strong zero-shot GPT-3 baseline, by over 4 absolute points in accuracy.

detailed.text_prompt:
Wenting Zhao, a PhD student at Cornell University, presented a paper titled "Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations". The paper focuses on the problem of identifying plausible explanations in abductive reasoning, a process that starts with a context and ends with an outcome, and seeks to bridge the gap between the two. Current approaches to this problem rely on supervised methods, which require the annotation of plausible explanations, a process that can be subjective and noisy. Zhao's paper answers the question of whether it is possible to learn abductive reasoning without supervision, and the answer is "Yes". 

The paper introduces an unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization). LiPoR treats explanations as a latent variable and maximizes the marginal likelihood of the outcome given the context, without needing to know which explanations are plausible. However, to ensure that the method prefers plausible explanations, an additional regularizer is needed. This regularizer, denoted by Omega, enforces the mutual exclusivity among explanations, meaning that if one explanation is true, the other cannot be true at the same time. 

The paper's results on the AlphaNLI dataset show that LiPoR outperforms other zero-shot models and the previous best unsupervised approach by over 4 absolute points in accuracy. The paper concludes that it is possible to learn abductive reasoning without supervision, and that the LiPoR method is effective in doing so. The paper can be found at tinyurl.com/zhao-lipor.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
The paper titled "Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations" by Wenting Zhao, a PhD student at Cornell University, presents a novel approach to abductive reasoning in a closed-world setting. The goal of abductive reasoning is to identify plausible explanations that bridge the information gap between a given context and an outcome. The paper introduces an unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization) to address the challenge of learning abductive reasoning without supervision regarding the plausibility of explanations.

The LiPoR method treats Explanation Z as a latent variable and aims to maximize the marginal likelihood of the Outcome Y given the Context X by marginalizing other possible explanations in Z. To achieve this, the paper introduces a regularizer that enforces mutual exclusivity among explanations. The regularizer, denoted by Omega, takes the maximum between the entropy of P of Z given XY and the log of M, where M is the number of plausible explanations. This regularizer helps to prefer a subset of explanations when the entropy of P of Z given XY is larger than the log of M.

The paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of LiPoR on the AlphaNLI dataset, which is a widely-used abductive reasoning dataset. The results show that LiPoR outperforms all other models, including a strong zero-shot GPT-3 baseline, by over 4 absolute points in accuracy. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the LiPoR method in learning abductive reasoning without supervision regarding the plausibility of explanations.

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel approach to abductive reasoning that leverages mutual exclusivity among explanations to learn plausible explanations in a closed-world setting. The LiPoR method demonstrates superior performance compared to existing models, highlighting its potential for advancing the field of abductive reasoning.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Title: Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations

Summary:
Wenting Zhao, a PhD student at Cornell University, presents a paper titled "Abductive Commonsense Reasoning Exploiting Mutually Exclusive Explanations." The paper introduces an unsupervised learning method called LiPoR (Likelihood Learning with Posterior Regularization) to address the challenge of learning abductive reasoning without supervision regarding the plausibility of explanations. The authors aim to identify plausible explanations in a closed-world setting, where a candidate set of explanations is given, and the goal is to find a subset of plausible explanations.

The paper begins with a concrete example to illustrate abductive reasoning, followed by a formal definition. In the example, the context is "Emily was stuck in traffic," and the outcome is "Emily made it to her flight." The set of possible explanations includes "Her flight was delayed" and "Her flight left on time." The goal of abductive reasoning is to identify a plausible explanation that bridges the information gap between the context and outcome. In this case, Explanation 1 is plausible as it provides the necessary missing information.

The authors propose LiPoR, which treats Explanation Z as a latent variable and maximizes the marginal likelihood of the Outcome Y given the Context X by marginalizing other possible explanations in Z. However, the unsupervised objective L only maximizes the likelihood of the outcome given the context and does not prefer plausible explanations. To address this, the authors introduce an additional regularizer, Omega, which enforces mutual exclusivity among explanations. The regularizer takes the max between the entropy of P of Z given XY and the log of M, where M is the number of plausible explanations. When the entropy of P of Z given XY is larger than the log of M, the regularizer minimizes the entropy, preferring a subset of explanations.

The paper presents results on the AlphaNLI dataset, comparing LiPoR to a number of zero-shot models and the previous best unsupervised approach. LiPoR outperforms all of them, including a strong zero-shot GPT-3 baseline, by over 4 absolute points in accuracy.

Conclusion:
The paper introduces LiPoR, an unsupervised learning method for abductive reasoning that exploits mutually exclusive explanations. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of LiPoR on the AlphaNLI dataset, outperforming existing approaches. This work contributes to the field of machine learning by providing a new approach to learn abductive reasoning without supervision regarding the plausibility of explanations.

ref: Pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable performance in image retrieval from text. However, their performance drops drastically when confronted with linguistically complex texts that they struggle to comprehend. Inspired by the Divide-and-Conquer algorithm and dual-process theory, in this paper, we regard linguistically complex texts as compound proposition texts composed of multiple simple proposition sentences and propose an end-to-end Neural Divide-and-Conquer Reasoning framework, dubbed NDCR. It contains three main components: 1) Divide: a proposition generator divides the compound proposition text into simple proposition sentences and produces their corresponding representations, 2) Conquer: a pretrained VLMs-based visual-linguistic interactor achieves the interaction between decomposed proposition sentences and images, 3) Combine: a neural-symbolic reasoner combines the above reasoning states to obtain the final solution via a neural logic reasoning approach. According to the dual-process theory, the visual-linguistic interactor and neural-symbolic reasoner could be regarded as analogical reasoning System 1 and logical reasoning System 2. We conduct extensive experiments on a challenging image retrieval from contextual descriptions data set. Experimental results and analyses indicate NDCR significantly improves performance in the complex image-text reasoning problem.

basic.text_prompt: Yunxin from Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, introduces a new work titled "A Neural Divide-and-Conquer Reasoning Framework for Image Retrieval from Linguistically Complex Text". The work addresses the challenge of retrieving images from complex text descriptions, which is difficult for typical visual language models. The proposed method, inspired by the Divide-and-Conquer strategy and Dual-Process Theory, decomposes complex propositions into simpler ones and integrates analogical and logical reasoning systems. The method consists of three modules: the Proposition Generator, the Visual-Linguistic Interactor, and the Neural-Symbolic Reasoner. The method outperforms other baselines and demonstrates effective processing interoperability. The author suggests that neural symbolic calculation and Divide-and-Conquer could improve the compositional reasoning and planning of large language models.

The proposed method combines Divide-and-Conquer strategy and Dual-Process Theory to improve the performance of visual language models. The method consists of three modules: Proposition Generator, Visual-Linguistic Interactor, and Neural-Symbolic Reasoner. The method outperforms other baselines and demonstrates the effectiveness of each module. The text concludes with suggestions for future research.

The speaker suggests that neural symbolic calculation and divide-and-conquer strategies could improve the compositional reasoning and planning of large language models.
short.m_audio_prompt: The proposed method, NDCR, is a neural divide-and-conquer reasoning framework for image retrieval from linguistically complex text. It addresses the challenge of retrieving images from complex text descriptions by decomposing the problem into simpler sub-problems and integrating the advantages of analogical reasoning (System 1) and logical reasoning (System 2). The method consists of three main modules: Proposition Generator, Visual-Linguistic Interactor, and Neural-Symbolic Reasoner. The experimental results show that NDCR outperforms other baselines, and the abolition experiments confirm the effectiveness of each module. The proposed method demonstrates interoperable processing and presents inference states and results in the middle step.

ref: Large language models (LLMs) that have been trained on multilingual but not parallel text exhibit a remarkable ability to translate between languages. We probe this ability in an in-depth study of the pathways language model (PaLM), which has demonstrated the strongest machine translation (MT) performance among similarly-trained LLMs to date. We investigate various strategies for choosing translation examples for few-shot prompting, concluding that example quality is the most important factor. Using optimized prompts, we revisit previous assessments of PaLM’s MT capabilities with more recent test sets, modern MT metrics, and human evaluation, and find that its performance, while impressive, still lags that of state-of-the-art supervised systems. We conclude by providing an analysis of PaLM’s MT output which reveals some interesting properties and prospects for future work.

The study systematically evaluates the model's translation capabilities using best practices from the machine translation community, including avoiding overlap between training and test data, and comparing against state-of-the-art systems. The researchers found that the quality of examples used for prompting is more important than the similarity to the source sentence, and that PaLM's fluency is comparable to other systems, but it tends to omit parts of the source sentence, leading to accuracy issues. The paper also provides recommendations for prompt selection strategies and highlights the importance of using high-quality translations for better results.

detailed.text_prompt:
David Vilar, a Google Translate researcher, presented a review of the paper "Prompting PaLM for Translation: Assessing Strategies and Performance." The paper, a joint work with his colleagues, is the first systematic study of large language model (LLM) prompting for machine translation. The authors evaluated the transition capability of LLMs using the best practices of the machine translation (MT) community, including the use of the latest test sets and comparison to state-of-the-art systems. They also used state-of-the-art, neural MT metrics and expert-based human evaluation results.

The authors found that the prompting strategy has a significant impact on the performance of LLMs for translation. In a simple experiment, they found that the form of the prompting can make a difference, with a difference of more than one BLEURT point observed in some cases. They settled on a 5-shot prompting strategy, where each sentence is marked with the language it's in. They found that the actual form of the prompting doesn't have a big influence in the case of several short promptings, but it's crucial for zero and one-shot prompting. The examples provided to the system carry most of the weight.

The authors also found that the quality of the examples is more important than the similarity to the source sentence. They compared the selecting prompts from the training data for the WMT evaluations on the dev data, which is more curated and of higher quality than the training data. They found that specialized state-of-the-art systems have a substantial advantage over PaLM translations, but PaLM comes pretty close to a commercial system.

The human evaluation using the MQM framework showed that the fluency of PaLM is comparable to state-of-the-art systems, but the main difference comes from the accuracy. The most common errors are omission errors, suggesting that PaLM sometimes chooses to produce a better-sounding translation by dropping parts of the source sentence that are made in translation. However, PaLM's "Style/Awkward" category is lower than for the state-of-the-art systems, indicating that PaLM provides really fluent output, but still with some problems of accuracy.

Summary of "Prompting PaLM for Translation: Assessing Strategies and Performance"

The paper "Prompting PaLM for Translation: Assessing Strategies and Performance" is a joint work with colleagues from Google Translate, focusing on the use of large language models (LLMs) like PaLM for machine translation (MT). PaLM, a 540 billion-parameter LLM, was trained on a vast collection of text and achieved state-of-the-art results in numerous NLP tasks.

The study presents the first systematic examination of LLM prompting for MT, evaluating the transition capability of these models using best practices from the MT community. This includes using the latest test sets to avoid overlap between the test data and the training data of the language model. The authors compared PaLM's performance to state-of-the-art systems, specifically the WMT evaluation, using state-of-the-art neural MT metrics and expert-based human evaluation results.

The research highlights the significant impact of prompting on LLM performance for translation. In experiments, the authors found that the quality of the examples provided to the system greatly influences the translation results, with a 5-shot prompting strategy showing minimal difference in performance compared to the actual form of the prompting. The study emphasizes the importance of selecting high-quality examples for zero and one-shot prompting, as well as five-shot prompting.

The paper concludes that the quality of the examples is more crucial than the similarity to the source sentence, and that selecting prompts from high-quality translations leads to better performance. The authors also note that specialized state-of-the-art systems have a substantial advantage over PaLM translations, but PaLM comes close to commercial systems in terms of fluency. The most common errors in PaLM's translations are omission errors, and the "Style/Awkward" category is lower for PaLM compared to state-of-the-art systems, indicating that PaLM provides fluent output with some accuracy issues.

In summary, the paper presents a comprehensive study on the use of PaLM for machine translation, highlighting the importance of prompting strategies and the quality of examples in achieving high translation performance. The research suggests that while PaLM is comparable to state-of-the-art systems in fluency, there are still accuracy issues that need to be addressed.

ref: Argument Quality Detection is an emerging field in NLP which has seen significant recent development. However, existing datasets in this field suffer from a lack of quality, quantity and diversity of topics and arguments, specifically the presence of vague arguments that are not persuasive in nature. In this paper, we leverage a combined experience of 10+ years of Parliamentary Debating to create a dataset that covers significantly more topics and has a wide range of sources to capture more diversity of opinion. With 34,890 high-quality argument-analysis pairs (a term we introduce in this paper), this is also the largest dataset of its kind to our knowledge. In addition to this contribution, we introduce an innovative argument scoring system based on instance-level annotator reliability and propose a quantitative model of scoring the relevance of arguments to a range of topics.

basic.text_prompt: Thea, a co-author of the paper "ArgAnalysis35K: A large-scale dataset for Argument Quality Analysis," explains the unique features of their dataset. The dataset, which contains 35,000 argument-analysis pairs, is the largest in its field and is sourced from high-quality speeches, expert debaters, and intermediate debaters. It also features a diverse range of arguments based on 24 themes, rather than a limited number of motions. The dataset introduces the concept of analysis, which combines claims, premises, and other elements to explain an argument better. It also includes instance-based annotator reliability, which allows for the utilization of annotations from annotators with human biases. Lastly, the dataset introduces a relevance model, which assigns a score to each argument's relevance to a particular theme. The dataset aims to provide a more diverse, reliable, and relevant set of arguments for argument quality analysis.

ref: Theory of Mind (ToM)—the ability to reason about the mental states of other people—is a key element of our social intelligence. Yet, despite their ever more impressive performance, large-scale neural language models still lack basic theory of mind capabilities out-of-the-box. We posit that simply scaling up models will not imbue them with theory of mind due to the inherently symbolic and implicit nature of the phenomenon, and instead investigate an alternative: can we design a decoding-time algorithm that enhances theory of mind of off-the-shelf neural language models without explicit supervision? We present SymbolicToM, a plug-and-play approach to reason about the belief states of multiple characters in reading comprehension tasks via explicit symbolic representation. More concretely, our approach tracks each entity’s beliefs, their estimation of other entities’ beliefs, and higher-order levels of reasoning, all through graphical representations, allowing for more precise and interpretable reasoning than previous approaches. Empirical results on the well-known ToMi benchmark (Le et al., 2019) demonstrate that SymbolicToM dramatically enhances off-the-shelf neural networks’ theory of mind in a zero-shot setting while showing robust out-of-distribution performance compared to supervised baselines. Our work also reveals spurious patterns in existing theory of mind benchmarks, emphasizing the importance of out-of-distribution evaluation and methods that do not overfit a particular dataset.

basic.text_prompt:
Melanie Sclar discusses her research on improving Theory of Mind reasoning skills in large language models (LLMs) through a method called SymbolicToM. Theory of Mind is the ability to understand the mental states of others, and it is traditionally measured in humans and LLMs through reading comprehension tasks involving multiple characters. Sclar's research focuses on addressing the poor performance of LLMs, such as ChatGPT and GPT-3, on false-belief tasks, which involve understanding characters' beliefs that may not match reality.

SymbolicToM is an inference-time method that uses explicit graphical representations to improve LLMs' Theory of Mind reasoning skills. It involves creating graphs that represent characters' beliefs and using these graphs to answer questions about characters' mental states. Sclar's research shows that SymbolicToM significantly improves the performance of LLMs on Theory of Mind tasks, outperforming supervised approaches and remaining effective on new datasets with linguistic diversity.

In conclusion, Sclar's research introduces SymbolicToM, a plug-and-play method that enhances LLMs' Theory of Mind reasoning skills, making them more interpretable and effective in understanding characters' beliefs. The method avoids overfitting risk and has shown significant performance gains in out-of-the-box LLMs, outperforming supervised approaches on out-of-domain story understanding and remaining beneficial on the new linguistic diversity dataset, ParaphrasedToMi.

ref: In everyday life, humans often plan their actions by following step-by-step instructions in the form of goal-oriented scripts. Previous work has exploited language models (LMs) to plan for abstract goals of stereotypical activities (e.g., “make a cake”), but leaves more specific goals with multi-facet constraints understudied (e.g., “make a cake for diabetics”). In this paper, we define the task of constrained language planning for the first time. We propose an over-generate-then-filter approach to improve large language models (LLMs) on this task, and use it to distill a novel constrained language planning dataset, Coscript, which consists of 55,000 scripts. Empirical results demonstrate that our method significantly improves the constrained language planning ability of LLMs, especially on constraint faithfulness. Furthermore, Coscript is demonstrated to be quite effective in endowing smaller LMs with constrained language planning ability.

basic.text_prompt: Siyu Yuan from Fudan University introduces their research on "Distilling Script Knowledge from Large Language Models for Constrained Language Planning." The study addresses the challenge of planning actions with specific constraints, such as "make a chocolate cake," which is less explored compared to planning for abstract goals. The researchers evaluate and improve the constrained language planning ability of large language models, finding that these models struggle with faithfulness to constraints. They propose an "over-generate-then-filter" method, which significantly enhances the quality of generated scripts. To facilitate research on constrained language planning, they create a dataset called CoScript, containing 55,000 specific goals and scripts. The dataset is designed to help smaller, specialized models perform better than large language models when trained on it. The research aims to advance the field by providing a valuable resource for further studies.

detailed.f_audio_prompt:
In this paper, Siyu Yuan from Fudan University introduces the work titled "Distilling Script Knowledge from Large Language Models for Constrained Language Planning". The paper focuses on the problem of constrained language planning, which involves planning actions with specific constraints, such as "make a chocolate cake". The authors aim to improve the constrained language planning ability of large language models and create a high-quality script dataset named CoScript.

The paper begins by discussing the importance of planning actions using step-by-step instructions in the form of goal-oriented scripts. Previous work has shown that large language models can effectively decompose goals into steps, but they mainly focus on abstract goals of stereotypical activities. The authors highlight the need for planning for specific goals with multi-faceted constraints, which remains under-studied.

To address this problem, the authors first evaluate and improve the constrained language planning ability of large language models. They use InstructGPT to extend abstract goals with multi-faceted constraints for human-in-the-loop data acquisition. The authors sample 100 specific goals and evaluate the scripts generated from large language models, finding that all language models achieve unsatisfactory results on planning for specific goals.

The authors then conduct a detailed analysis to investigate why learning models fail. They find that the semantic completeness in generated scripts is acceptable, but the faithfulness to the constraints cannot be guaranteed. The authors explore a more fine-grained topic categories of constraints defined in wikiHow and find that the planning performance of InstructGPTs varies considerably for goals of different categories.

To improve generation quality, the authors adopt the idea of over-generate-then-filter. They first show constraint types with examples for InstructGPT and obtain specific goals based on the seed abstract goals. Then, InstructGPT over-generates K scripts for specific goals. A filter model is developed to select the faithful scripts. The authors convert scripts and goals into InstructGPT embeddings and calculate the cosine similarity as similarity scores to measure semantic similarity. They reward the script that contains the keywords of the target constraint and keep the script if the target goal scores the highest in the goal set.

The authors' method greatly improves the planning ability both in semantic completeness and faithfulness to the constraint. They create the CoScript dataset, which contains 55,000 specific goals with scripts. The authors find that CoScript shows high pluralism in the generated specific goals. They find that T5 fine-tuned on CoScript can generate scripts of higher quality than most large language models, indicating that smaller models can surpass larger models when properly trained on suitable datasets.

In summary, the paper establishes the constrained language planning problem, evaluates the constrained language planning ability of large language models, develops an over-generate-then-filter method for large language models, and creates the CoScript dataset for constrained language planning. The authors hope that the CoScript dataset will be a valuable resource to advance research on language planning.

The study focuses on the ability of large language models to plan for specific goals, such as "make a chocolate cake," which is not well-studied in previous research. The authors define the problem of constrained language planning and highlight the need for good planners to write scripts that are both reasonable and faithful to constraints.

To evaluate the constrained language planning ability of large language models, the authors first improve the models' performance. However, they find that the models still struggle with planning for specific goals. The authors conduct a detailed analysis to understand why the models fail and identify that the semantic completeness in generated scripts is acceptable, but faithfulness to constraints cannot be guaranteed.

The authors propose an over-generate-then-filter method to improve the generation quality of the scripts. They first obtain specific goals based on seed abstract goals and then use InstructGPT to over-generate K scripts for specific goals. A filter model is developed to select the faithful scripts, and the authors use cosine similarity and keyword rewards to measure semantic similarity and select the most faithful scripts.

The authors also create a dataset called CoScript, which consists of 55,000 specific goals with scripts. They use crowd-sourced workers to ensure the quality of the validation and test sets. The CoScript dataset shows high pluralism in the generated specific goals and can be used to train smaller but specialized models for constrained language planning.

The study concludes that the CoScript dataset can be a valuable resource for advancing research on language planning.
