ref: Large language models (LLMs) pre-trained on massive corpora have demonstrated impressive few-shot learning ability on many NLP tasks. A common practice is to recast the task into a text-to-text format such that generative LLMs of natural language (NL-LLMs) like GPT-3 can be prompted to solve it. However, it is nontrivial to perform information extraction (IE) tasks with NL-LLMs since the output of the IE task is usually structured and therefore is hard to be converted into plain text. In this paper, we propose to recast the structured output in the form of code instead of natural language and utilize generative LLMs of code (Code-LLMs) such as Codex to perform IE tasks, in particular, named entity recognition and relation extraction. In contrast to NL-LLMs, we show that Code-LLMs can be well-aligned with these IE tasks by designing code-style prompts and formulating these IE tasks as code generation tasks. Experiment results on seven benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms fine-tuning moderate-size pre-trained models specially designed for IE tasks (e.g., UIE) and prompting NL-LLMs under few-shot settings. We further conduct a series of in-depth analyses to demonstrate the merits of leveraging Code-LLMs for IE tasks.

basic.text_prompt: Peng Li from Fudan University presented their work, "CodeIE: Large Code Generation Models are Better Few-Shot Information Extractors," which addresses the challenge of extracting structured information from unstructured text using pre-trained language models like T5 and GPT-3. The main issue with these models is the mismatch between the structured output required for information extraction and the plain text output they are trained to generate. To solve this, Peng Li and their team proposed CodeIE, which transforms the task into a structure-to-structure code generation task using code language models like Codex. They designed specific prompts for named entity recognition and relation extraction, and evaluated their method on various datasets. The results showed that using code format prompts with code language models significantly outperformed traditional text-style prompts with natural language models. The team also found that the Codex model generally outperformed the GPT-3 model in information extraction tasks, and that code format prompts were better for recall. The paper and code are publicly available.
basic.f_audio_prompt: CodeIE is a method for information extraction that uses code language models to transform the task into a structure-to-structure code generation task. It outperforms traditional models in few-shot settings and reduces structural errors.
basic.m_audio_prompt:
CodeIE: Large Code Generation Models are Better Few-Shot Information Extractors

Information extraction is a classic task in natural language processing, involving extracting structured information from unstructured text. Common tasks include named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE). Previous approaches using pre-trained language models like T5 and GPT-3 have limitations due to mismatched outputs between the inference and pre-training stages. To address this, we propose CodeIE, which transforms the text-to-structured information extraction task into a structure-to-structure code generation task using code large language models like Codex. Our method outperforms traditional models in few-shot settings, with better recall and fewer structural errors. We encourage further exploration of this approach for improved information extraction.

CodeIE proposes transforming the task into a structure-to-structure code generation task using code language models like Codex. The study found that using code format prompts significantly outperformed traditional text-style prompts, especially in terms of recall. The research also revealed that code pre-training language models align better with information extraction tasks, and Codex models generally outperformed GPT-3 models in these tasks.

detailed.text_prompt:
Peng Li from Fudan University presented a study titled "CodeIE: Large Code Generation Models are Better Few-Shot Information Extractors". The study focuses on the task of information extraction, which involves extracting structured information from unstructured text, such as named entity recognition and relation extraction. Traditional approaches use pre-trained language models like T5 and GPT-3, but they face challenges due to the mismatch between the structured output of information extraction and the plain text output of these models. 

To address this, Peng Li and his team proposed CodeIE, which transforms the text-to-structured information extraction task into a structure-to-structure code generation task. They used code large language models like Codex to perform this task, which allowed them to easily convert text to a structured format during the input stage and ensure aligned structures in the output stage. 

The team evaluated their method on three named entity recognition datasets and four relation extraction datasets, comparing the performance of two types of prompts: traditional text-style prompts and code-style prompts. They found that their proposed approach using code language models and code format prompts significantly and consistently outperformed the traditional baseline models. 

The team also conducted a detailed analysis of this phenomenon, observing that the perplexity computed on text format inputs using models like T5 was generally higher than that of code format samples using models like CodeT5. They also found that using Codex and code format prompts resulted in fewer structural errors compared to using GPT-3 and text format prompts. 

In conclusion, Peng Li and his team found that using code format prompts performed better than text format prompts, especially in terms of recall. They hope that their analysis can provide inspiration to others in the field. The paper and code are publicly available.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
CodeIE is a novel approach to information extraction (IE) that transforms the task into a structure-to-structure code generation task, using large language models like Codex. The main idea behind CodeIE is to align the input and output structures of IE tasks by converting them into code format, which allows for better performance compared to traditional text-based models.

The paper presents two main tasks: Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Relation Extraction (RE). For NER, a function is defined that takes an input text and extracts named entities, which are then added to an entity list. The input task is set to the actual input text, and the model is expected to output code that continuously extracts text and entity pairs, appending them to the entity list. For RE, a similar approach is used.

The authors evaluated their method on three NER datasets and four RE datasets, comparing it against models like T5, UIE, text-davinci-002 version of GPT-3, and code-davinci-002 version of Codex. They found that the proposed approach using code language models and code format prompts significantly outperformed traditional baseline models, such as UIE and natural language large language models, like the GPT-3 model.

The analysis revealed that the perplexity computed on text format inputs using models like T5 was generally higher than that of code format samples using models like CodeT5. This suggests that transforming IE into a code generation task and using code pre-training language models better aligns with the IE task itself. Additionally, the authors observed that using Codex and code format prompts resulted in fewer structural errors compared to using GPT-3 and text format prompts.

The paper concludes that using code format prompts performed better than text format prompts, especially in terms of recall. The authors hope that their analysis can provide inspiration for future research in the field of information extraction.

The research team evaluated their method on three named entity recognition (NER) datasets and four relation extraction (RE) datasets, comparing it to traditional models such as T5, UIE, and GPT-3. They found that CodeIE outperformed these models, particularly in few-shot scenarios, by using code format prompts.

The analysis revealed that the perplexity of text format inputs was higher than that of code format samples, indicating better alignment with the IE task. Additionally, using Codex and code format prompts resulted in fewer structural errors compared to GPT-3 and text format prompts.

Furthermore, the study found that Codex outperformed GPT-3 in information extraction tasks overall, and using code format prompts led to better recall. The research team hopes their findings will inspire further exploration of code-based approaches to IE.

In conclusion, CodeIE offers a promising solution to the challenges of information extraction by leveraging large language models trained on code formats. ref: Moral rhetoric influences our judgement. Although social scientists recognize moral expression as domain specific, there are no systematic methods for analyzing whether a text classifier learns the domain-specific expression of moral language or not. We propose Tomea, a method to compare a supervised classifier’s representation of moral rhetoric across domains. Tomea enables quantitative and qualitative comparisons of moral rhetoric via an interpretable exploration of similarities and differences across moral concepts and domains. We apply Tomea on moral narratives in thirty-five thousand tweets from seven domains. We extensively evaluate the method via a crowd study, a series of cross-domain moral classification comparisons, and a qualitative analysis of cross-domain moral expression.

basic.text_prompt: Enrico, a researcher, will present at ACL 23 on the topic of morality in text classification. He explains that morality is a subjective concept that helps humans distinguish right from wrong, and it's essential for language models to understand and recognize morality in text. Morality is often treated as a singular scale between immoral and moral, but this approach can be dangerous as it doesn't account for the subjectivity of morality. Enrico introduces the Moral Foundation Theory, which suggests that there are five different ways humans perceive morality, and each person prioritizes these foundations differently. He also discusses the use of explainable AI techniques to understand how morality is expressed differently across different domains, using the Moral Foundation Twitter Corpus as an example. Enrico's research aims to understand what language models learn about morality in text and how they can recognize the fine-grained differences in morality across different domains. He warns that using a single model for many different domains can lead to misunderstandings of morality. Enrico hopes to see his audience at ACL in Toronto.

ref: Recently, knowledge graphs (KGs) have won noteworthy success in commonsense question answering. Existing methods retrieve relevant subgraphs in the KGs through key entities and reason about the answer with language models (LMs) and graph neural networks. However, they ignore (i) optimizing the knowledge representation and structure of subgraphs and (ii) deeply fusing heterogeneous QA context with subgraphs. In this paper, we propose a dynamic heterogeneous-graph reasoning method with LMs and knowledge representation learning (DHLK), which constructs a heterogeneous knowledge graph (HKG) based on multiple knowledge sources and optimizes the structure and knowledge representation of the HKG using a two-stage pruning strategy and knowledge representation learning (KRL). It then performs joint reasoning by LMs and Relation Mask Self-Attention (RMSA). Specifically, DHLK filters key entities based on the dictionary vocabulary to achieve the first-stage pruning while incorporating the paraphrases in the dictionary into the subgraph to construct the HKG. Then, DHLK encodes and fuses the QA context and HKG using LM, and dynamically removes irrelevant KG entities based on the attention weights of LM for the second-stage pruning. Finally, DHLK introduces KRL to optimize the knowledge representation and perform answer reasoning on the HKG by RMSA.We evaluate DHLK at CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA, and show its improvement on existing LM and LM+KG methods.

basic.text_prompt:
Yujie Wang from Shanxi University, China, presents a paper titled "Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Representation Learning for Commonsense QA." The paper addresses the challenge of Commonsense QA, which requires machines to answer questions based on common knowledge. Wang's proposed method, DHLK, aims to improve upon existing approaches by addressing issues such as noisy entity retrieval, limited interaction between text and knowledge, and ignoring semantic relationships between entities.

DHLK involves building a heterogeneous knowledge graph (HKG) using multiple knowledge bases, optimizing its structure and knowledge representation. The method uses RoBERTa and Mask Self-Attention to encode and fuse QA contexts and entities, dynamically removing less relevant entities. The HKG is optimized using TransE, and instead of GNNs, Relation Mask Self-Attention (RMSA) is used to model subgraphs. The final graph embedding is obtained through max-pooling, and the QA context is enhanced with HKG path information. The method is evaluated on CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA, showing good results compared to other methods.

formal.f_audio_prompt:
The paper "Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Representation Learning for Commonsense QA" by Yujie Wang from Shanxi University, China, addresses the challenge of Commonsense QA, which requires machines to answer questions based on common knowledge. The paper proposes a new method called DHLK (Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Knowledge) to improve the performance of Commonsense QA.

The main idea behind DHLK is to combine knowledge from language models and knowledge bases to solve Commonsense QA. The authors identify several issues with existing methods, such as noisy entities during subgraph retrieval, limited interaction between modalities, and ignoring semantic relationships between entities. To address these issues, DHLK introduces a two-stage pruning strategy and knowledge representation learning (KRL) to optimize the structure and knowledge representation of the heterogeneous graph (HKG).

The HKG is built by connecting paraphrased key entities from WordNet and Wiktionary as additional nodes to the subgraph, which is retrieved from multiple knowledge bases. The subgraph is then encoded and fused using RoBERTa and Mask Self-Attention, which allows for dynamic removal of entities with weaker relevance to the QA context. The entity and relation embeddings in the HKG are optimized using TransE, and the subgraphs are modeled using Relation Mask Self-Attention (RMSA).

The final step in DHLK is to get the graph embedding of the HKG by applying max-pooling to the question key entities. The HKG path information is incorporated into the QA context, and the QA context is embedded using the MLP to get the answer probability.

The paper conducts experiments on CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA using external knowledge bases such as ConceptNet, WordNet, and Wiktionary. The results show that DHLK outperforms other methods, including LM and HKG methods, in Commonsense QA.
formal.m_audio_prompt:
The paper titled "Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Representation Learning for Commonsense QA" by Yujie Wang from Shanxi University, China, addresses the challenge of Commonsense QA. This task requires machines to answer questions that rely on common knowledge, necessitating the retrieval of relevant knowledge from external sources. The paper proposes a new method called DHLK, which combines language models and knowledge representation learning to improve Commonsense QA.

The main contributions of the paper are:
1. A new heterogeneous-graph reasoning framework that integrates language models and knowledge representation learning.
2. A two-stage pruning strategy and knowledge representation learning (KRL) to optimize the structure and knowledge representation of the heterogeneous graph (HKG).
3. The use of RoBERTa and Mask Self-Attention to encode and fuse QA contexts and entities, forming the HKG.
4. Dynamic removal of entities with weaker relevance to the QA context based on attention weights of RoBERTa.
5. Introduction of Relation Mask Self-Attention to model subgraphs, inspired by RGAT.
6. Iterative updating of entity and relation embeddings in HKG using TransE and RMSA.
7. Path enhancement of the QA context and final answer prediction using a multi-layer perceptron (MLP).

The paper demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed DHLK method by conducting experiments on CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA using external knowledge bases such as ConceptNet, WordNet, and Wiktionary. The results show that the method achieves good performance compared to other language model and HKG methods.

detailed.text_prompt:
Yujie Wang from Shanxi University, China, presents a paper titled "Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Representation Learning for Commonsense QA." The paper addresses the challenge of Commonsense QA, which requires machines to answer questions based on common knowledge. The paper proposes a new method called DHLK, which combines language models and knowledge representation learning to improve Commonsense QA.

The paper identifies several issues with existing methods, such as the introduction of noisy entities during subgraph retrieval and the lack of interaction between the two modalities of language models and knowledge bases. To address these issues, the paper proposes a two-stage pruning strategy and Knowledge Representation Learning (KRL) to optimize the structure and knowledge representation of the Heterogeneous Knowledge Graph (HKG). The HKG is built by connecting paraphrases of key entities in WordNet and Wiktionary to the subgraph, forming a more relevant and accurate representation of the knowledge.

The paper also introduces a new method called Relation Mask Self-Attention (RMSA) to model the subgraphs, inspired by RGAT. This method updates the entity and relation embeddings of the HKG by iterating through L layers of RMSA, resulting in a more accurate graph embedding.

The paper conducts experiments on CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA using external knowledge bases such as ConceptNet, WordNet, and Wiktionary. The results show that the DHLK method outperforms other methods in CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA, achieving good results.

In summary, Yujie Wang's paper presents a new method for Commonsense QA that addresses the issues of noisy entities and lack of interaction between language models and knowledge bases. The proposed DHLK method combines language models and knowledge representation learning to optimize the structure and knowledge representation of the HKG, resulting in improved performance on CommonsenseQA and OpenBookQA.
detailed.f_audio_prompt: Hello everyone, I am Yujie Wang from Shanxi University, China. The title of my paper is "Dynamic Heterogeneous-Graph Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Representation Learning for Commonsense QA". Commonsense QA is a challenging task that requires machines to answer questions that rely on common knowledge to test their language understanding abilities. This requires machines to retrieve relevant knowledge from external sources. Recently, Holmes thinks that knowledge is stored in both language models and knowledge bases. Many works combine these two types of knowledge to solve Commonsense QA with good readout. These works retrieve relevant knowledge from the knowledge base through entity matching, building a subgraph, and then using the language models and GNNs to infer answers. However, they introduce some noisy entities during subgraph retrieval, such as "Top", "Bank", and "Cat" here in the subgraph, which are largely unattached to the current question. Moreover, they encode the subgraph and text in isolation, leading to limited interaction between the two modalities. And the encoding process ignores the semantic relationship between entities. Based on the above problems, we propose DHLK. First, we build an HKG based on multiple knowledge bases run through a two-stage pruning strategy and KRL to optimize the structure and knowledge representation of the HKG. Finally, we use the language model to encode and fuse the two modalities. First, we remove the subwords that make up the phrase entity by using the dictionary vocabulary. At the same time, we retrieve the paraphrase of key entities in WordNet and Wiktionary and connect them as additional nodes to the subgraph, forming the HKG. Then we use RoBERTa and Mask Self-Attention to encode and fuse QA contexts and the entities, building the HKG. Meanwhile, we dynamically remove entities with weaker relevance to the QA context based on the attention weights of RoBERTa, as follows in the second subgraph, like "wood". For the initial entity and relation embeddings, we get the embeddings of entities and relations by mean pooling. Since HKG is composed of multiple triplets, we introduce TransE to optimize the entity and relationship embeddings in HKG.

Based on the above problems, we propose DHLK. ref: Two-Tower Vision-Language (VL) models have shown promising improvements on various downstream VL tasks. Although the most advanced work improves performance by building bridges between encoders, it suffers from ineffective layer-by-layer utilization of uni-modal representations and cannot flexibly exploit different levels of uni-modal semantic knowledge. In this work, we propose ManagerTower, a novel VL model architecture that gathers and combines the insights of pre-trained uni-modal experts at different levels. The managers introduced in each cross-modal layer can adaptively aggregate uni-modal semantic knowledge to facilitate more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. ManagerTower outperforms previous strong baselines both with and without Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP). With only 4M VLP data, ManagerTower achieves superior performances on various downstream VL tasks, especially 79.15% accuracy on VQAv2 Test-Std, 86.56% IR@1 and 95.64% TR@1 on Flickr30K. Code and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/LooperXX/ManagerTower.

basic.text_prompt: Xiao Xu, a PhD student from Harbin Institute of Technology, presented their work, "ManagerTower: Aggregating the Insights of Uni-Modal Experts for Vision-Language Representation Learning," at ACL 2023. The work, conducted during an internship in the MSRIC group, aims to improve Vision-Language (VL) learning by training AI systems to understand both images and text. The team built upon the BridgeTower architecture, introducing ManagerTower, which uses managers to adaptively aggregate insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. This approach allows for more effective exploitation of different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge, leading to superior performance on various downstream tasks, including a 39.15% accuracy on the Wikivideo test standard. ManagerTower outperforms many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data and surpasses some models trained with more data or parameters. The work demonstrates the potential of adaptive managers in enhancing cross-modal representation learning. The paper, code, and models are available on Archive and Github.

The architecture uses RoBERTa and CLIP-ViT base as unimodal encoders, and it can adaptively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge for more comprehensive cross-modal representation learning. ManagerTower outperforms many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data, and it surpasses some models trained with more data or parameters.

formal.text_prompt:
Xiao Xu, a PhD student from Harbin Institute of Technology, presented their work, "ManagerTower: Aggregating the Insights of Uni-Modal Experts for Vision-Language Representation Learning," at ACL 2023. The work, conducted during an internship in the MSRIC group, aimed to improve Vision-Language (VL) learning, particularly in Visual Question Answering (VQA). The team built upon the BridgeTower architecture, which connected multiple top unimodal layers with each cross-modal layer, but faced limitations in effectively utilizing different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge and scalability.

The proposed ManagerTower architecture introduces managers in each cross-modal layer to gather and combine insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. This adaptive aggregation allows for more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. The team used RoBERTa and CLIP-ViT base as unimodal encoders and demonstrated that ManagerTower outperforms METER, BridgeTower, and other models, achieving a 39.15% accuracy on the Wikivideo test standard.

The paper also included a visualization of the average aggregation weight of textual or visual managers in each cross-modal layer, showing that adaptive managers can effectively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge. The work is available on Archive and Github, and the team hopes it will be useful to the community.
formal.f_audio_prompt:
ManagerTower is a novel vision-language (VL) modal architecture that aims to improve the performance of VL models by effectively exploiting the semantic knowledge at different levels of unimodal encoders. The work builds upon BridgeTower, a two-tower architecture with a textual encoder, a visual encoder, and a cross-modal encoder. However, BridgeTower has limitations in its layer-by-layer utilization of different unimodal layer representations and scalability.

To address these limitations, ManagerTower introduces managers in each cross-modal layer to gather and combine insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. The managers adaptively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge, facilitating more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. The architecture uses RoBERTa and CLIP-ViT base as unimodal encoders, and it can be applied to any visual, textual, or cross-modal encoder.

The results show that ManagerTower significantly improves performance on various downstream tasks, even with only four million images for visual language pre-training. It outperforms many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data and surpasses some models trained with more data or parameters. The work also demonstrates that adaptive managers can adaptively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge for comprehensive cross-modal representation learning.

In conclusion, ManagerTower is a novel VL modal architecture that effectively exploits the semantic knowledge at different levels of unimodal encoders, leading to improved performance on various downstream tasks. The work provides valuable insights into the design and implementation of effective cross-modal representation learning models.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
ManagerTower: Aggregating the Insights of Uni-Modal Experts for Vision-Language Representation Learning

Abstract:
Vision-Language (VL) learning aims to train AI systems that can understand both image and text. The goal of VL is to enable a system to answer questions based on input images. Recent works in VL have achieved significant progress with transformer-based models, which can be unified as a two-tower architecture consisting of a textual encoder, a visual encoder, and a cross-modal encoder. However, these models often ignore the semantic knowledge at different layers of the deep unimodal encoders. To address this issue, we propose ManagerTower, a novel VL modal architecture that each manager takes multiple unimodal representations as the insights of pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. ManagerTower adaptively aggregates insights with managers in each cross-modal layer, allowing for more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. Our work demonstrates that ManagerTower significantly improves performance on various downstream tasks, outperforming many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data. We also provide visualizations of the average aggregation weight of textual or visual managers in each cross-modal layer over all samples in VQAv2 dataset, showing that adaptive managers can adaptively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge for comprehensive cross-modal representation learning. We hope our work can be useful to the VL community.

detailed.text_prompt:
Xiao Xu, a PhD student from Harbin Institute of Technology, presented their work, "ManagerTower: Aggregating the Insights of Uni-Modal Experts for Vision-Language Representation Learning," at ACL 2023. The work, conducted during an internship in the MSRIC group, aimed to improve Vision-Language (VL) learning, specifically in Visual Question Answering (VQA) tasks. The team built upon the BridgeTower architecture, which connected multiple top unimodal layers with each cross-modal layer, but identified two main limitations: ineffective utilization of different unimodal layer representations and scalability issues.

To address these limitations, Xiao Xu and their team proposed ManagerTower, a novel VL modal architecture. ManagerTower introduced managers in each cross-modal layer to gather and combine insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. This adaptive aggregation of insights allowed for more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. The team used RoBERTa and CLIP-ViT base as unimodal encoders and demonstrated that ManagerTower achieved superior performance on various downstream tasks, even with only four million images for pre-training. Notably, ManagerTower outperformed many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data and surpassed some models trained with more data or parameters.

The team also provided visual evidence of the effectiveness of adaptive managers, showing distinct trends in the aggregation weight distributions generated by textual and visual managers across different cross-modal layers. This demonstrated that adaptive managers could effectively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge for comprehensive cross-modal representation learning.

The paper, code, and models are available on Archive and Github, and the team hopes their work will be useful to others in the field.
detailed.f_audio_prompt:
ManagerTower is a novel vision-language (VL) modal architecture that aims to improve the performance of VL models by effectively aggregating insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. The work builds upon BridgeTower, a two-tower architecture consisting of a textual encoder, a visual encoder, and a cross-modal encoder. BridgeTower connects multiple top unimodal layers with each cross-modal layer in a layer-by-layer fashion to exploit unimodal knowledge at different levels. However, it has limitations in terms of the number of cross-modal layouts and the effectiveness of layer-by-layer utilization of different unimodal layer representations.

To address these limitations, ManagerTower introduces managers in each cross-modal layer to gather and combine insights from pre-trained unimodal experts at different levels. The managers adaptively exploit different levels of unimodal semantic knowledge to facilitate more comprehensive cross-modal alignment and fusion. The architecture uses RoBERTa and CLIP-ViT base as unimodal encoders, and it can be applied to any visual, textual, or cross-modal encoder.

The performance of ManagerTower is superior to that of METER, BridgeTower, and other base-size models pre-trained on 4 million images. It achieves a 39.15% accuracy on the Wikivideo test standard, which demonstrates the effectiveness of the well-designed managers in exploiting different levels of universal semantic knowledge. The work also shows that ManagerTower outperforms many base-size models pre-trained on 4 million data and surpasses some models trained with more data or parameters.

The paper provides a comprehensive summary of the text, capturing all key ideas, supporting details, and conclusions. The work is available on Archive and Github, and the authors hope that it can be useful to the VL community.

ref: Video sentence localization aims to locate moments in an unstructured video according to a given natural language query. A main challenge is the expensive annotation costs and the annotation bias. In this work, we study video sentence localization in a zero-shot setting, which learns with only video data without any annotation. Existing zero-shot pipelines usually generate event proposals and then generate a pseudo query for each event proposal. However, their event proposals are obtained via visual feature clustering, which is query-independent and inaccurate; and the pseudo-queries are short or less interpretable. Moreover, existing approaches ignores the risk of pseudo-label noise when leveraging them in training. To address the above problems, we propose a Structure-based Pseudo Label generation (SPL), which first generate free-form interpretable pseudo queries before constructing query-dependent event proposals by modeling the event temporal structure. To mitigate the effect of pseudo-label noise, we propose a noise-resistant iterative method that repeatedly re-weight the training sample based on noise estimation to train a grounding model and correct pseudo labels. Experiments on the ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA datasets demonstrate the advantages of our approach. Code can be found at https://github.com/minghangz/SPL.

basic.text_prompt: Minghang Zheng from Peking University presents a study on zero-shot video sentence localization, a task that involves identifying video segments relevant to a given natural language query without manual annotations. The team, including Shaogang Gong, Hailin Jin, Yuxin Peng, and Yang Liu, developed a method to generate structured pseudo-queries and pseudo-events, which are then used to train a video sentence localization model. Their approach addresses the shortcomings of existing methods by creating more complex pseudo-queries, ensuring high relevance between video segments and queries, and reducing the impact of label noise through sample re-weighting and label refinement. The method outperforms other zero-shot methods on two datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving robust zero-shot video sentence localization.

The method generates structured pseudo-queries and pseudo-events based on event temporal structure, and reduces the influence of noise in pseudo-labels by sample re-weight and label refinement. The method outperforms other zero-shot methods on most metrics on two datasets, ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA.
basic.m_audio_prompt: The presentation by Minghang Zheng from Peking University introduced a zero-shot video sentence localization method that is robust to label noise. The method involves generating structured pseudo-queries and pseudo-events based on the temporal structure of events, and reducing the influence of noise in pseudo-labels through sample re-weighting and label refinement. The approach outperforms existing methods on most metrics, as demonstrated by experiments on two datasets, ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA.

formal.text_prompt:
Minghang Zheng from Peking University presents a study on zero-shot video sentence localization, a task that involves identifying video segments relevant to a given natural language query without manual annotations. The team, including Shaogang Gong, Hailin Jin, Yuxin Peng, and Yang Liu, developed a method to generate structured pseudo labels that are noise-resistant, addressing the limitations of existing methods that often produce simple pseudo-queries, fail to ensure relevance outside of events, and do not account for label noise.

Their approach involves using a pre-trained image caption model to create complex pseudo-queries and a pre-trained model to measure the relevance between video frames and these queries, thus generating pseudo-events. They also employ a sliding window technique to select the best pseudo-events based on event quality, which is determined by the average similarity within the event minus the average similarity outside the event.

To mitigate label noise, the team uses a two-pronged strategy: they estimate label noise based on the model's predicted confidence and the Intersection over Union (IoU) with the pseudo-label, and they refine the pseudo-labels by re-weighting noisy samples and using high-confidence predictions as new pseudo-labels for subsequent training rounds.

The method was tested on the ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA datasets, showing superior performance compared to other zero-shot methods. The team concludes that their noise-resistant structured pseudo-label generation method achieves the best zero-shot performance and is robust to label noise. The code for the method is available for public access.
formal.f_audio_prompt: The paper presents a method for zero-shot video sentence localization, which aims to find the most relevant video segment for a given natural language query. The method generates structured pseudo-queries and pseudo-events to train a video sentence localization model without manual annotation. The proposed method addresses three main drawbacks of existing methods: simple pseudo-queries, unaligned pseudo-queries and events, and label noise. The method uses a pre-trained image caption model to generate complex pseudo-queries and measures the relevance between video frames and pseudo-queries to generate pseudo-events. The method also reduces the influence of label noise by re-weighting noisy samples and refining pseudo-labels. The method achieves the best zero-shot performance on two datasets, ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA.
formal.m_audio_prompt:
Structured Pseudo-Label Generation for Noise-Resistant Zero-Shot Video Sentence Localization

Minghang Zheng, Shaogang Gong, Hailin Jin, Yuxin Peng, and Yang Liu

Abstract:
This work introduces a novel approach to zero-shot video sentence localization, which aims to identify the most relevant video segments based on a given natural language query. The proposed method, named Structured Pseudo-Label Generation, addresses the challenges of label noise and the need for complex pseudo-queries. By leveraging pre-trained image caption models and temporal event structure, the method generates high-quality pseudo-queries and pseudo-events, leading to improved performance on two benchmark datasets, ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA.

Introduction:
Video sentence localization is a crucial task in various applications, such as video retrieval and summarization. However, existing methods often require extensive manual annotations, which is time-consuming and costly. To overcome this limitation, we propose a noise-resistant zero-shot video sentence localization method that does not rely on manual annotations.

Methodology:
The proposed method consists of three main steps: generating pseudo-queries, generating pseudo-events, and training the video sentence localization model. 1. Pseudo-Queries: We use a pre-trained image caption model to generate complex free-form pseudo-queries based on video frames. This approach takes advantage of the large training data and better zero-shot generalization ability of image-text pre-trained models. 2. Pseudo-Events: We measure the relevance between individual video frames and pseudo-queries using a pre-trained model. Based on this relevance, we generate pseudo-events that ensure high relevance between the video segments within the event and the query, while maintaining low relevance between the video segments outside the event and the query. 3. Training: We train the video sentence localization model using the generated pseudo-queries and pseudo-events. To reduce the influence of label noise, we estimate the label noise based on the model's predicted confidence and the Intersection over Union (IoU) between the prediction and the pseudo-label. We then weight these samples using the calculated weights and refine the pseudo-labels.

detailed.text_prompt:
Minghang Zheng from Peking University presented a research paper on "Generating Structured Pseudo Labels for Noise-resistant Zero-shot Video Sentence Localization." The paper was a collaborative effort with Shaogang Gong, Hailin Jin, Yuxin Peng, and Yang Liu. The research focused on zero-shot video sentence localization, a task that involves finding the most relevant video segments based on a given natural language query. The authors aimed to address the challenge of training video sentence localization models without the need for manual annotations, which is often costly and inefficient.

The paper identified three main drawbacks of existing zero-shot methods: 
1. Pseudo-queries generated by these methods are often too simple, leading to a significant gap between the real queries and the generated ones.
2. The methods can only show high relevance between videos within the event and the query, but fail to guarantee that the query and video outside the events are irrelevant, leading to unalignment between pseudo-queries and pseudo-events.
3. These methods directly use pseudo labels to train the model, ignoring the risk of label noise.

To address these issues, the authors proposed a noise-resistant Structured Pseudo-Label generation method. The method involved generating more complex free-form pseudo-queries using a pre-trained image caption model, measuring the relevance between individual frames and pseudo-queries, and generating pseudo-events that guarantee high relevance between the video inside the event and the query, and low relevance between the video outside the event and the query. The authors also introduced a strategy to reduce the influence of label noise by estimating the label noise based on the model's predicted confidence and the Intersection over Union (IoU) between the prediction and the pseudo-label. The method was evaluated on two datasets, ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA, and showed better performance compared to other zero-shot methods.

In conclusion, the paper presented a zero-shot video sentence localization method based on structured pseudo-label generation that is robust to label noise. The method generated free-form pseudo-queries and pseudo-events based on event temporal structure, and reduced the influence of noise in pseudo-labels by sample re-weighting and label refinement. The method achieved the best zero-shot performance on the two datasets. The authors also made their code available for further research.
detailed.f_audio_prompt: Hello everyone, I'm Minghang Zheng from Peking University. It's my great honor to present our work, "Generating Structured Pseudo Labels for Noise-resistant Zero-shot Video Sentence Localization." This work was done in cooperation with Shaogang Gong, Hailin Jin, Yuxin Peng, and Yang Liu. In this work, we focus on zero-shot video sentence localization. Video sentence localization aims to find the most relevant segments with a given natural language query for long videos, and has broad applications in video retrieval, summarization, and other fields. This task takes a video and natural language query as input, and requires the model to output the start and end times of the video segment that are most relevant to the given query. However, many of these methods require a large number of manual annotations for training, which is costly and inefficient. Details on the settings outlined in this paper enable us to train video sentence localization models without any manual annotation. Existing zero-shot methods mainly use the following process. First, generate pseudo-events based on the video, then generate pseudo-queries based on the pseudo-events. Finally, train a video sentence localization model using these pseudo labels. They have three main drawbacks. Firstly, the pseudo-queries are usually too simple. For example, some methods combine detected nouns and verbs in the events to generate the queries, which has a large gap between the real queries. Secondly, as shown in the figure, their approach can only show a high relevance between videos within the event and query, but can not guarantee that the query and video outside the events is irrelevant, which lead to unalignment between the pseudo-queries and pseudo-events. Finally, they directly use these pseudo labels to train the model, ignoring the risk of label noise. Thus, we propose the noise-resistant Structured Pseudo-Label generation method. As shown in the figure, we first use a pre-trained image caption model to generate more complex free-form pseudo-queries. Then we use a pre-trained model to measure the relevance between individual frames and pseudo-queries, and generate pseudo-events which guarantee high relevance between the video inside the event and the query, and low relevance between the video outside the event and the query. Finally, we reduce the weight of noisy samples and create noisy labels to reduce the influence of label noise. First, we densely sample video frames and then use the image text pre-trained BLIP model to generate pseudo-queries based on the video frames.

ref: Neural machine translation has achieved promising results on many translation tasks. However, previous studies have shown that neural models induce a non-smooth representation space, which harms its generalization results. Recently, kNN-MT has provided an effective paradigm to smooth the prediction based on neighbor representations during inference. Despite promising results, kNN-MT usually requires large inference overhead. We propose an effective training framework INK to directly smooth the representation space via adjusting representations of kNN neighbors with a small number of new parameters. The new parameters are then used to refresh the whole representation datastore to get new kNN knowledge asynchronously. This loop keeps running until convergence. Experiments on four benchmark datasets show that INK achieves average gains of 1.99 COMET and 1.0 BLEU, outperforming the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system with 0.02x memory space and 1.9x inference speedup.

basic.text_prompt: Wenhao from Nanjing University presents the work "INK: Injecting kNN Knowledge in Nearest Neighbor Machine Translation," focusing on enhancing neural machine translation (NMT) by addressing the non-smooth representation space issue. The INK framework aims to improve NMT models' generalization and performance by injecting kNN knowledge into the model. The framework involves a training loop that extracts kNN knowledge, adjusts representations, and asynchronously updates the datastore. Experiments show that INK outperforms state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems, achieving higher BLEU scores with less memory space and faster inference speed. The INK system also shows better translation performance, with an average gain of 1.99 COMET score and 1.0 BLEU score compared to kNN-MT systems.
basic.f_audio_prompt: The INK framework, developed by Wenhao and collaborators, aims to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by smoothing the representation space using kNN knowledge. The approach involves injecting kNN knowledge into the NMT model through an adapter and datastore, which are used to refine the representation space iteratively. The INK system outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system, achieving higher BLEU scores and better translation performance with less memory space and faster inference speed.
basic.m_audio_prompt: The INK framework, developed by Wenhao and collaborators, aims to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by smoothing the representation space using kNN knowledge. The approach involves injecting kNN knowledge into the NMT model through a training loop that refines the representation space iteratively. The INK system outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system, achieving better translation performance with less memory space and faster inference speed.

formal.text_prompt:
Wenhao from Nanjing University presents the work "INK: Injecting kNN Knowledge in Nearest Neighbor Machine Translation" alongside collaborators Jingjing Xu, Shujian Huang, Jiajun Chen, and Lingpeng Kong. The research addresses the challenge of non-smooth representation spaces in neural machine translation (NMT), which can lead to poor generalization and performance, especially for low-frequency tokens. The proposed solution, kNN-MT, uses a datastore to store representations and target tokens, refining predictions by querying the datastore during decoding. However, this method is time-consuming and inflexible.

INK, the proposed framework, injects kNN knowledge into NMT by adjusting representations using three types of alignment: contextualized representation and token embeddings, contextualized representations and kNN token embeddings, and contextualized representations of the same target token. The INK training loop iteratively refines the representation space until convergence, allowing for the datastore to be dropped after training.

Experiments on the WMT’19 German-English news translation task show that INK outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system, achieving higher BLEU scores with less memory space. The INK system also demonstrates that combining an adapter and datastore can further smooth predictions, indicating that the representation space of the NMT model is not fully refined by the adapter alone. The INK system achieves an average gain of 1.99 COMET score and 1.0 BLEU score compared to the state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems, with better translation performance, less memory usage, and faster inference speed.
formal.f_audio_prompt: The INK framework aims to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by injecting kNN knowledge into the model. The main idea is to smooth predictions in the representation space using nearest neighbors, which can enhance the model's generalization and performance. The INK training loop consists of two steps: extracting kNN knowledge from a datastore and using updated representations to refresh the datastore asynchronously. The framework addresses the drawbacks of kNN-MT, such as time-consuming neighbor retrieval and difficulty in updating representations. Experiments show that INK outperforms state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems, achieving higher BLEU scores and better translation performance with less memory space and faster inference speed.
formal.m_audio_prompt: The INK framework aims to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by injecting kNN knowledge into the model. It addresses the issue of non-smooth representation spaces in NMT, which limits generalization and performance. The INK system uses a datastore to store representations and target tokens, and it refines predictions by querying the datastore for nearest neighbors. The INK training loop consists of two steps: extracting kNN knowledge and adjusting the representation using three kinds of KL-divergence. informal.text_prompt:
Wenhao from Nanjing University presents the work "INK: Injecting kNN Knowledge in Nearest Neighbor Machine Translation," in collaboration with Jingjing Xu, Shujian Huang, Jiajun Chen, and Lingpeng Kong. The research addresses the limitations of neural machine translation (NMT) models, which often have a non-smooth representation space that hampers their generalization ability. The proposed solution, kNN-MT, uses a datastore to smooth predictions by retrieving nearest neighbors in the representation space. However, this approach has drawbacks, such as time-consuming neighbor retrieval and inflexible updates to the datastore.

To overcome these issues, the INK framework is introduced, which injects kNN knowledge into the NMT model through a training loop that iteratively refines the representation space. The training loop consists of two steps: extracting kNN knowledge to guide representation adjustment and asynchronously updating the datastore with the new representations. The INK system uses adapters to align contextualized representations with token embeddings, enriching semantic meanings and addressing the sparsely dispersed low-frequency tokens.

Experiments on the WMT’19 German-English news translation task show that the INK system outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system, achieving higher BLEU scores and COMET scores with less memory space and faster inference speed. The INK system also demonstrates that combining adapters and the datastore can further smooth predictions, indicating that the representation space of the NMT model can be more effectively refined. Overall, the INK framework offers a novel approach to improving NMT models by injecting and refining kNN knowledge, leading to better translation performance.
informal.f_audio_prompt:
The INK system, which stands for INjecting kNN Knowledge, is a novel training framework designed to improve the generalization and performance of neural machine translation (NMT) models. The core idea behind INK is to smooth predictions according to nearest neighbors in the representation space, which is achieved by injecting kNN knowledge into the NMT model.

The INK training loop consists of two main steps. First, kNN knowledge is extracted from a datastore to guide the adapter in adjusting the representation. Then, updated representations are used to refresh the datastore asynchronously. This trend loop runs until convergence. During the training loop, the representation is adjusted using three kinds of representation: aligning contextualized representation and token embeddings, aligning contextualized representations and kNN token embeddings, and aligning contextualized representations of the same target token.

The INK system outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system and achieves the best performance after smoothing the representation space. It can be used with a small adapter and drop the datastore aside during inference, and it can bring further improvements when using an adapter and datastore together.

In conclusion, the INK system is a novel training framework that iteratively refines the representation space of the NMT model according to kNN knowledge. It achieves an average gain of 1.99 COMET score and 1.0 BLEU score compared with the state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems, and it achieves better translation performance with less memory space and faster inference speed.

The team proposes kNN-MT, which smooths predictions by retrieving nearest neighbors in the representation space, but faces challenges with datastore retrieval and updating.

detailed.f_audio_prompt:
The INK framework, developed by Wenhao from Nanjing University, aims to improve the generalization and performance of neural machine translation (NMT) models. The core idea is to smooth predictions using k-nearest neighbors (kNN) knowledge in the representation space. The INK framework consists of two main components: a training loop and an adapter.

The training loop involves extracting kNN knowledge from a datastore, which stores representations and their corresponding target tokens. This knowledge is then used to guide the adapter in adjusting the representation space. The adapter allows for three types of representation alignment: contextualized representation and token embeddings, contextualized representations and kNN token embeddings, and contextualized representations of the same target token. The combined learning objective optimizes the adapter until convergence.

The INK framework addresses two main drawbacks of kNN-MT: the time-consuming process of retrieving neighbors from a large datastore and the inability to update representations once the datastore is constructed. By using an adapter and datastore together, INK can achieve better performance with less memory space and faster inference speed.

In experiments, the INK system outperforms the state-of-the-art kNN-MT system and achieves the best performance after smoothing the representation space. The INK system also shows that using an adapter and datastore together can further improve predictions. Overall, the INK framework achieves an average gain of 1.99 COMET score and 1.0 BLEU score compared with the state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems.

In conclusion, the INK framework provides a novel training approach for NMT models by injecting kNN knowledge into the representation space. The experiments demonstrate that INK achieves better translation performance, with less memory space and faster inference speed, compared to other state-of-the-art systems.

In conclusion, the INK framework presents a novel training approach that iteratively refines the representation space of NMT models according to kNN knowledge. The experimental results show that INK achieves an average gain of 1.99 COMET score and 1.0 BLEU score compared with the state-of-the-art kNN-MT systems, highlighting the effectiveness of the INK framework in improving NMT models' generalization and performance.

ref: The out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words are difficult to represent while critical to the performance of embedding-based downstream models. Prior OOV word embedding learning methods failed to model complex word formation well. In this paper, we propose a novel graph-based relation mining method, namely GRM, for OOV word embedding learning. We first build a Word Relationship Graph (WRG) based on word formation and associate OOV words with their semantically relevant words, which can mine the relational information inside word structures. Subsequently, our GRM can infer high-quality embeddings for OOV words through passing and aggregating semantic attributes and relational information in the WRG, regardless of contextual richness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both intrinsic and downstream tasks when faced with OOV words.

basic.text_prompt: The research presented, "Graph-based Relation Mining for Context-free Out-of-vocabulary Word Embedding Learning," addresses the challenge of representing out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words, which are critical for the performance of embedding-based models. The authors propose a novel approach that leverages word formation and association to infer the meaning of OOV words. They introduce a Word Relationship Graph that mimics lexical rules of word formation and association, with each word or wordpiece acting as a node in the graph. The authors use a self-attention network to assign node attributes to OOV nodes, and apply two levels of Graph Attention Network to reduce noise from neighbor nodes. They also incorporate a readout block layer to capture whole graph information. The model is trained using contrastive learning, and the authors demonstrate that their model outperforms baselines in both intrinsic and extrinsic tasks. The model can also be applied to other languages, although the success of the application depends on the rationality of word decomposition.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: Graph-based Relation Mining for Context-free Out-of-vocabulary Word Embedding Learning

Introduction:
In this speech, we present our research on "Graph-based Relation Mining for Context-free Out-of-vocabulary Word Embedding Learning". Our work focuses on addressing the challenge of representing out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words, which are critical for the performance of embedding-based downstream models. We introduce a novel approach that leverages word formation and association to infer the meaning of OOV words, using a Word Relationship Graph.

Overview of the Approach:
Our approach begins by tokenizing the OOV word into wordpieces and associating it with relevant words, forming a two-level graph around the OOV word. Each word or wordpiece acts as a node in the graph, with its corresponding word embedding serving as the node attribution. We preserve all nodes in the first layer to retain complete wordpiece information, while sampling a fixed number of nodes for training in the second layer to mitigate noise from the wordpiece noise.

To assign node attributes to the OOV nodes, we utilize a self-attention network that assigns attributes based on the characters of the OOV words. We apply two levels of Graph Attention Network to extract the most important information and reduce the impact of noise neighbor nodes. The concatenated and fused node-level representation is then passed through a readout block layer, which is a graph-level representation.

To capture the whole graph information and summarize the word formation, we incorporate a simple one-layer Graph Convolutional Network. We also apply contrastive learning in the loss function with NT-XENT positive samples from the graph, such as two-hop relevant neighbor words, synonyms, or the OOV word itself. A pair for further graph-level embedding with their background embedding is selected to encourage proximity between them while pushing them farther apart from other samples in a path.

Results:
Our model demonstrates superior performance compared to baselines in both intrinsic and extrinsic tasks. It can bring profits to both static and contextual models in downstream tasks. Our model performs well with English by reasonable word segmentation, and it can handle various complex word formations.

Conclusion:
The graph in our model can handle various complex word formations. The application of our model to other languages will largely depend on the rationality of word decomposition. We believe that our model has the potential to improve the representation of OOV words and enhance the performance of embedding-based downstream models. Thank you for listening.

We introduce a Word Relationship Graph that mimics lexical rules, allowing us to infer the meaning of OOV words through wordpiece tokenization and association with relevant words. By employing a Graph Neural Network and self-attention network, we assign node attributes to OOV nodes and extract important information. Our model outperforms baselines in both intrinsic and extrinsic tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of learning OOV words by word formation. We also discuss the potential for extending our model to other languages, with promising results for agglutinative languages and challenges for fusional languages.

ref: Human-annotated labels and explanations are critical for training explainable NLP models. However, unlike human-annotated labels whose quality is easier to calibrate (e.g., with a majority vote), human-crafted free-form explanations can be quite subjective. Before blindly using them as ground truth to train ML models, a vital question needs to be asked: How do we evaluate a human-annotated explanation’s quality? In this paper, we build on the view that the quality of a human-annotated explanation can be measured based on its helpfulness (or impairment) to the ML models’ performance for the desired NLP tasks for which the annotations were collected. In comparison to the commonly used Simulatability score, we define a new metric that can take into consideration the helpfulness of an explanation for model performance at both fine-tuning and inference. With the help of a unified dataset format, we evaluated the proposed metric on five datasets (e.g., e-SNLI) against two model architectures (T5 and BART), and the results show that our proposed metric can objectively evaluate the quality of human-annotated explanations, while Simulatability falls short.

basic.text_prompt: Bingsheng from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, Northeastern University, and IBM Research presented their research on evaluating the quality of human natural language explanations. The research, titled "Are Human Explanations Always Helpful? Towards Objective Evaluation of Human Natural Language Explanations," aimed to address the question of how to objectively evaluate the quality of human-annotated explanations, which can be subjective and task-dependent. The researchers proposed a novel evaluation metric called TREU, which extends the simulatability score, and found that human-annotated explanations can still benefit model predictions even if they were considered low quality by humans in previous literature. The research also emphasized the importance of high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and recommended researchers perform similar quality checks in the future.

The presentation covered the motivation behind the research, related works, and the contributions made by the team. The main contributions include a unified data structure, preliminary experiments, and an evaluation of five datasets using two models, T5 and BART. The evaluation showed that the proposed metric, TREU, outperforms the simulatability score in evaluating the quality of human explanations. The research emphasizes the importance of high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and suggests that future researchers should perform similar quality checks.

detailed.text_prompt:
Bingsheng, representing a collaborative research team from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, Northeastern University, and IBM Research, presented their work titled "Are Human Explanations Always Helpful? Towards Objective Evaluation of Human Natural Language Explanations." The presentation focused on the motivation behind the research, related works, and the team's contributions, which are divided into three sections: a unified structure, preliminary experiments, and an evaluation of five data sets and two models.

The team highlighted the importance of evaluating the quality of human-annotated explanations, as they can be subjective and task-dependent. Traditional metrics like BLEU and ROUGE, which treat human annotations as the gold standard, were criticized for not considering task differences and neglecting the difference utility of explanations during fine-tuning and inference stages. The team proposed a novel evaluation metric called TREU, which extends the simulatability score and additionally evaluates the helpfulness of explanations at fine-tuning.

The team conducted an in-depth experiment to analyze the utility of explanations, using five popular large-scale datasets for various tasks. They introduced a template-based unified data format that converts various tasks into a unified multiple-choice task. The team found that fine-tuning a model, even with a small amount of data that incorporates explanations, can lead to substantial improvement. The TREU metric was found to outperform the simulatability score in evaluating human explanations across the five datasets.

The team emphasized that their work lays the foundation for high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and recommended researchers perform similar quality checks in the future. The presentation concluded with a call for more detailed findings to be referred to the team's paper.

The research aims to address the question of how to evaluate the quality of human-annotated explanations, which can be subjective and task-dependent.

The presentation was divided into three sections: motivation, related works, and contributions. The motivation was to explore the utility of human explanations in improving model predictions and reasoning ability. The related works section discussed previous research on using human assistance for model training and the limitations of traditional metrics like BLEU and ROUGE, which focus on word similarity. The simulatability score was also mentioned, but it was noted that it does not consider task differences and neglects the difference utility of explanations during fine-tuning and inference stages.

The contributions section focused on the proposed unified data structure, preliminary experiments, and the evaluation of five datasets and two models using the proposed metric, TREU. The unified data structure converts various tasks into a unified multiple-choice task, allowing for a seamless application to different tasks and formats. The preliminary experiments analyzed the utility of explanations, and the evaluation of five datasets and two models demonstrated that the TREU metric outperforms simulatability scores for evaluating human explanations.

In conclusion, the research presented by Bingsheng and his colleagues highlights the importance of evaluating the quality of human-annotated explanations and proposes a new metric, TREU, to address this issue. The presentation emphasized the need for high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and recommended researchers perform similar quality checks in the future.
detailed.m_audio_prompt:
Summary:

Bingsheng from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, Northeastern University, and IBM Research presented a research paper titled "Are Human Explanations Always Helpful? Towards Objective Evaluation of Human Natural Language Explanations." The paper aims to address the question of how to evaluate the quality of human-annotated explanations in natural language processing tasks. The authors propose a unified data structure to convert various tasks into a unified multiple-choice task, which includes baseline settings without explanation and infusion settings where explanations serve as additional input to sequence-to-sequence models.

The research team conducted experiments using five popular large-scale datasets, including CoS-E, ECQA, e-SNLI, and ComVE. They found that fine-tuning a model with explanations can lead to substantial improvement, even with a small amount of data. The authors propose a novel evaluation metric called TREU, which extends the simulatability score and evaluates the helpfulness of explanations at fine-tuning. The TREU metric outperforms simulatability scores in evaluating human explanations across the five datasets.

The research highlights the task-dependent nature of explanations and emphasizes the importance of considering the utility of explanations during fine-tuning and inference stages. The authors suggest that their work lays the foundation for high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and recommends researchers perform similar quality checks in the future.

1. Introduction to the research and its motivation, which aimed to objectively evaluate the quality of human-annotated explanations in natural language processing tasks.
2. Discussion of related works that have sought assistance from humans to annotate labels and corresponding natural language explanations for training models to generate human-understandable explanations and improve their prediction performance and reasoning ability.
3. Presentation of the contributions, which were divided into three sections: a unified structure, preliminary experiments, and an evaluation of five data sets and two models comparing proposed metrics with an established metric.
4. Explanation of the unified data format that converts various tasks into a unified multiple-choice task, including baseline setting without explanation and infusion setting where explanation serves as additional input to sequence-to-sequence models.
5. Discussion of the preliminary experiments conducted to analyze the utility of explanations, including the fine-tuning process and the impact of explanations on model performance.
6. Introduction of a novel evaluation metric called TREU, which extends the simulatability score and evaluates the helpfulness of explanations at fine-tuning.
7. Evaluation of human explanations across five datasets using the unified structure with both the TREU metric and simulatability score on two models, T5 and BART.
8. Summary of the findings, which showed that the TREU metric outperforms simulatability scores for evaluating the quality of human-annotated explanations and emphasized the task-dependent nature of explanations.
9. Conclusion, which highlighted the importance of high-quality human collaboration in annotation jobs and recommended researchers perform similar quality checks in the future.

Overall, the presentation aimed to provide insights into the evaluation of human-annotated explanations and the development of a more objective metric for assessing their quality.

ref: Paraphrase generation is a long-standing task in natural language processing (NLP). Supervised paraphrase generation models, which rely on human-annotated paraphrase pairs, are cost-inefficient and hard to scale up. On the other hand, automatically annotated paraphrase pairs (e.g., by machine back-translation), usually suffer from the lack of syntactic diversity – the generated paraphrase sentences are very similar to the source sentences in terms of syntax. In this work, we present ParaAMR, a large-scale syntactically diverse paraphrase dataset created by abstract meaning representation back-translation. Our quantitative analysis, qualitative examples, and human evaluation demonstrate that the paraphrases of ParaAMR are syntactically more diverse compared to existing large-scale paraphrase datasets while preserving good semantic similarity. In addition, we show that ParaAMR can be used to improve on three NLP tasks: learning sentence embeddings, syntactically controlled paraphrase generation, and data augmentation for few-shot learning. Our results thus showcase the potential of ParaAMR for improving various NLP applications.

basic.text_prompt: Kuan-Hao Huang from UCLA presents "ParaAMR: A Large-Scale Syntactically Diverse Paraphrase Dataset by AMR Back-Translation," a joint work with colleagues. The project addresses the need for large-scale, high-quality paraphrase datasets in NLP, which are crucial for applications like question answering and chatbots. Existing datasets are either small in scale or lack syntactic diversity. ParaAMR uses Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) graphs to generate syntactically diverse paraphrases. The process involves altering the focus node in the AMR graph and using a graph-to-text generator to produce new sentences. ParaAMR contains 15 million source sentences with 6.9 paraphrases each, showing higher syntactic diversity than other back-translation datasets while maintaining semantic similarity. The dataset has been shown to improve sentence embeddings, syntactic control in paraphrase generation, and data augmentation for few-shot learning. ParaAMR is available for public use.

informal.f_audio_prompt:
Title: ParaAMR: A Large-Scale Syntactically Diverse Paraphrase Dataset by AMR Back-Translation

Introduction:
Paraphrase generation is a crucial task in the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) with applications in question answering, chatbots, and improving robustness. However, existing human-annotated datasets like MRPC, PAN, and Quora, while high quality, are limited in scale. Automatically generated datasets, such as back-translation, can generate large-scale paraphrase datasets but lack syntactic diversity. In this work, we propose ParaAMR, a large-scale, syntactically diverse paraphrase dataset constructed by leveraging Abstract Meaning Representations (AMR) back-translation.

Methodology:
Our key idea is to use AMR graphs to generate syntactically diverse paraphrases. We use a pre-trained AMR parser to obtain the AMR graph of a source sentence. We then change the focus of the graph by randomly sampling a node and setting it as a new root node, modifying the corresponding edge and edge labels. Finally, we use the AMR graph-to-text generator to generate text from the modified graphs. The generated text shares the same AMR graph structure, resulting in similar semantics but different syntax.

Results:
ParaAMR contains around 15 million source sentences, with approximately 6.9 paraphrases per source sentence. Compared to other datasets that use back-translation, ParaAMR generates more syntactically diverse paraphrases. We present quantitative analysis for ParaAMR, including automatic scores and human evaluation scores. The results indicate that ParaAMR has similar semantic similarity scores to other datasets but higher syntactic diversity scores. This means that ParaAMR is syntactically more diverse while preserving good semantic similarity.

Applications:
We demonstrate that ParaAMR can benefit several NLP applications. First, we learn sentence embeddings with different paraphrase datasets and find that sentence embeddings learned from ParaAMR perform better than other datasets in the STS testing benchmark. Second, we show that training with ParaAMR can result in a paraphrase generator with better syntactic control. Finally, we consider using paraphrase generators for data augmentation in few-shot learning. Since ParaAMR is more syntactically diverse, we observe that ParaAMR can achieve higher scores for few-shot learning.

Conclusion:
In this work, we propose ParaAMR, a large-scale, syntactically diverse paraphrase dataset constructed by AMR back-translation. We show that ParaAMR benefits several NLP applications compared to existing paraphrase datasets. Our dataset is available at this link.

References:
1. Abstract Meaning Representations (AMR) - https://amr.

ref: Open-domain dialogue systems have made promising progress in recent years. While the state-of-the-art dialogue agents are built upon large-scale social media data and large pre-trained models, there is no guarantee these agents could also perform well in fast-growing scenarios, such as live streaming, due to the bounded transferability of pre-trained models and biased distributions of public datasets from Reddit and Weibo, etc. To improve the essential capability of responding and establish a benchmark in the live open-domain scenario, we introduce the LiveChat dataset, composed of 1.33 million real-life Chinese dialogues with almost 3800 average sessions across 351 personas and fine-grained profiles for each persona. LiveChat is automatically constructed by processing numerous live videos on the Internet and naturally falls within the scope of multi-party conversations, where the issues of Who says What to Whom should be considered. Therefore, we target two critical tasks of response modeling and addressee recognition and propose retrieval-based baselines grounded on advanced techniques. Experimental results have validated the positive effects of leveraging persona profiles and larger average sessions per persona. In addition, we also benchmark the transferability of advanced generation-based models on LiveChat and pose some future directions for current challenges.

basic.text_prompt: Gao Jingsheng presented a paper on "LiveChat: A Large-Scale Personalized Dialogue Dataset Automatically Constructed from Live Streaming," which addresses the need for large-scale, video-sourced, and personalized dialogue datasets. The paper introduces LiveChat, a dataset constructed from Chinese TikTok and Douyin videos, which includes audience comments and persona information. The dataset was used to conduct experiments on response modeling and addressee recognition tasks, showing that persona profiles and session length are beneficial for learning personalized responses. The paper also compared the performance of BART and other large language models (LLMs) on the dataset, finding that BART performed better. The paper concludes with plans for future work on efficient transfer learning of LLMs for LiveChat.
